limitations: The AI still faces challenges when handling multiple separate responses from the same person or when questions overlap across different psychological domains. Furthermore, ensuring the AI can effectively explain exactly why it made a certain diagnosis remains a complex hurdle.

title: How Visually Literate Are Large Language Models? Reflections on Recent Advances and Future Directions
summary: Artificial intelligence models are now being used to create and read data visualizations, prompting the question of whether they are truly visually literate. While AI can easily write code to generate a chart or answer basic questions about a picture, it is unclear if it actually comprehends complex data the way a human analyst does. This study tested leading AI models against strict benchmarks to measure their ability to synthesize visual information and detect misleading designs. The goal was to understand where AI excels in data visualization and where it falls dangerously short.
methodology: The researchers evaluated advanced multimodal models (like GPT-4), which can look at images alongside text. They subjected the AI to a suite of specific task sets designed to test human visual literacy. The AI was asked to look at various charts and perform tasks ranging from identifying high-level trends to extracting precise numerical data, allowing researchers to grade the AI's actual comprehension of the visual evidence.
findings: The results were mixed. The AI excelled at recognizing overarching trends, spotting extreme high or low values, and understanding general visualization best practices. However, the models completely struggled with basic data extraction; if the raw numbers weren't provided, the AI failed to accurately retrieve precise values from the chart. It also had a hard time distinguishing between different colors and occasionally invented fake data (hallucination).
conclusion: While Large Language Models represent a massive technological leap for helping users create charts using everyday language, they are not yet fully reliable for reading and analyzing them. They can act as excellent assistants for spotting broad patterns, but human oversight is still absolutely required for precise, analytical tasks.
limitations: The primary limitations of current multimodal AI are its inconsistency, inability to accurately differentiate colors, and a dangerous tendency to hallucinate incorrect visual evidence. Additionally, these models lack the ability to integrate real-world historical context into their visual analysis.

title: Power Grid Model Generation Based on the Tool-Augmented Large Language Model
summary: To study and improve electrical power systems, scientists need detailed digital models of power grids. However, real-world power grid blueprints are highly classified for national security reasons. This forces researchers to artificially create fake grid models to run their experiments, which is traditionally a very difficult and slow process. This paper solves this problem by using a "Tool-Augmented" Large Language Model. By treating grid generation as a structured text problem, the researchers created an AI tool where users can simply type what kind of grid they need, and the AI automatically builds the complex mathematical model.
methodology: Because power grid configurations are essentially stored as organized text files, the researchers framed the creation process as a "structured text generation" task. They connected a Large Language Model to specialized engineering tools (tool-augmentation). An engineer can type a prompt in normal, natural language specifying the exact topologies and power flow properties they need. The AI then translates those human instructions into the rigid, structured data formats required for power system simulations.
findings: The numerical results proved that the tool-augmented AI could successfully and consistently generate synthetic power grid models. The generated digital grids strictly adhered to the complex physical requirements, topologies, and power flow rules that the users requested in their initial natural language prompts.
conclusion: Using tool-augmented AI completely revolutionizes how electrical engineers conduct research. Instead of manually coding synthetic grids or lacking data due to security restrictions, researchers now have an intuitive, highly effective tool to instantly generate custom, physically accurate grid models just by typing what they need.
limitations: While the AI generates text-based models well, ensuring the absolute physical viability and stability of extremely massive or highly unconventional grid structures may still require secondary validation by traditional engineering software.

title: REaMA: Building Biomedical Relation Extraction Specialized Large Language Models Through Instruction Tuning
summary: Medical research contains millions of connections, such as which genes cause which diseases, but there are too many papers for humans to read. This task of finding connections is called Biomedical Relation Extraction. General AI models (like standard ChatGPT) struggle with this because medical jargon is incredibly complex and requires deep domain knowledge. To fix this, researchers built specialized AI models called REaMA. They achieved this by taking general AI models and heavily training them on a massive, highly specific medical rulebook, teaching the AI exactly how to read and extract clinical relationships from dense medical texts.
methodology: The researchers created an enormous training set called "REInstruct," which contains one hundred and fifty thousand high-quality examples of medical instructions and their correct answers. They used a technique called "instruction tuning" to feed this data into open-source AIs of varying sizes (7 billion and 13 billion parameters). They also trained the AI to use "Chain of Thought" reasoning, forcing the computer to think through the medical connections step-by-step rather than just guessing the final answer.
findings: The specialized REaMA models performed exceptionally well across seven different medical testing databases. The larger 13-billion parameter model was remarkably successful, beating the current best-in-class automated methods on five out of the seven tests. The inclusion of the step-by-step reasoning significantly boosted the AI's ability to handle brand new, unseen medical texts.
conclusion: You cannot rely on a general, out-of-the-box AI to understand complex biomedical literature. However, by aggressively "instruction tuning" open-source models with massive amounts of tailored medical data, scientists can create incredibly powerful, specialized tools that automate the extraction of life-saving medical knowledge from millions of documents.
limitations: Even specialized models are limited by their overall size (parameters), meaning smaller models still struggle to capture the most complex medical language patterns. Additionally, processing the ever-increasing volume of diverse global medical literature requires immense computational resources.

title: Verification is All You Need: Prompting Large Language Models for Zero-Shot Clinical Coding
summary: This paper shows that LLMs are much better at checking (verifying) medical codes than at creating them from scratch. Instead of asking the LLM to invent ICD codes for a patient note, the system gives it a short list of possible codes and asks it to confirm which ones are correct. This simple change turns a hard generation task into an easier verification task and dramatically improves accuracy.
methodology: Clinical notes are fed to a traditional model (PLM-ICD) to produce a small set of candidate ICD codes. These candidates plus the original note are put into a carefully designed prompt for an LLM. The prompt asks the LLM to (1) find evidence in the note for each candidate, then (2) decide yes/no. Three prompt styles were tested: plain verification, chain-of-thought verification, and evidence-based verification. Experiments used GPT-4o, Llama 3.1, Qwen2, and other models on MIMIC-IV and CodiEsp datasets.
findings: GPT-4o with evidence-based verification reached micro-F1 of 0.4428 on MIMIC-IV (vs. 0.2401 for direct generation) and 0.3037 zero-shot on CodiEsp (far above traditional models). LLMs showed excellent cross-institution, cross-language, and cross-ICD-version generalization. Even reasoning models like DeepSeek-R1 improved when switched from generation to verification. Larger models generally performed better, but verification helped even small models.
conclusion: LLMs are poor at freely generating long lists of ICD codes but excel when given candidates to verify. The verification paradigm plus evidence-based prompting makes zero-shot clinical coding accurate, interpretable, and highly generalizable across hospitals, languages, and coding systems—without any task-specific training.
limitations: Still relies on a traditional model to generate the initial candidate list; if that list is poor, verification suffers. Tested mainly on English and Spanish notes; performance on other languages or extremely rare codes is unproven. Oracle sampling (perfect candidates) was used only for ablation, not in real deployment.

title: Recent Advances in Interactive Machine Translation With Large Language Models
summary: This survey reviews how Large Language Models are transforming interactive machine translation. Instead of one-shot translation, LLMs now work with humans in real time—accepting feedback, following instructions, and refining outputs on the fly. The paper organizes recent research into nine key directions and shows how LLMs are making translation faster, more accurate, and more adaptable, especially for low-resource languages.
methodology: The authors systematically reviewed recent papers on LLMs for machine translation, focusing on interactive aspects. They grouped findings into nine research directions: in-context learning, chain-of-thought prompting, base model pre-training, translation fine-tuning, LLMs as scorers, post-editing, interpretability, model decoding, and assessment. For each direction they summarize key techniques, experimental setups, and performance gains across models like GPT-4, Llama, BLOOM, and PaLM.
findings: LLMs excel in interactive settings: in-context examples and chain-of-thought prompting dramatically boost quality; fine-tuning with translation memories or human feedback further improves domain adaptation and low-resource performance; post-editing with LLMs reduces human effort; and new decoding methods plus evaluation paradigms make outputs more controllable and measurable. Models like GPT-4 and Llama 3.1 achieve competitive or superior results compared with traditional NMT while supporting real-time human interaction.
conclusion: Interactive LLMs represent a major shift in machine translation—from static pipelines to collaborative human-AI systems. The nine directions covered provide a practical roadmap for building more accurate, efficient, and user-friendly translation tools that can handle diverse languages and domains with minimal additional training.
limitations: Most results are still on high-resource language pairs; low-resource performance, while improved, remains inconsistent. Real-time interaction adds latency and cost. Many studies use proprietary models (GPT-4) or large open models that are expensive to run. Ethical concerns around bias, privacy, and over-reliance on LLM outputs are noted but not fully resolved.

title: APMSR: an intelligent QA system for synthetic biology empowered by adaptive prompting and multi-source knowledge retrieval
summary: Synthetic biology researchers struggle with fragmented, fast-changing knowledge scattered across papers, databases, and the web. This paper introduces APMSR, a smart QA system that first figures out the question type (multiple-choice or true/false) and picks the best prompt template automatically. It then uses the LinUCB algorithm to choose the most useful retrieval path—local database, external web search, or both—balancing new information with proven sources. Tested on Zymomonas mobilis questions, it delivers far more accurate answers than plain LLMs or standard RAG.
methodology: APMSR uses a 'prompt-in-prompt' step: an LLM classifies the question type and selects a matching template from a pool. For retrieval, three paths (local BM25+vector search, external web search, hybrid with RRF fusion) are treated as 'arms' in a multi-armed bandit problem. LinUCB scores each path using semantic relevance and past reward feedback (correctness), then picks the best one. The chosen context plus the adaptive prompt goes to the LLM for the final answer. Experiments ran on DeepSeek R1 and other models.
findings: On synthetic-biology multiple-choice and true/false questions, APMSR reached 93% accuracy. It improved average accuracy by 39% over plain LLM and 13% over standard RAG. Adaptive prompting and LinUCB path selection contributed most. DeepSeek R1 + APMSR performed best overall. The system handled emerging research topics better by dynamically pulling external sources.
conclusion: APMSR shows that combining question-aware prompting with smart multi-source retrieval makes LLMs much more reliable for specialized scientific QA. The system is practical, scalable, and significantly reduces hallucinations in fast-moving fields like synthetic biology, providing researchers with trustworthy, up-to-date answers.
limitations: Tested mainly on one model organism (Zymomonas mobilis); broader synthetic-biology topics need further validation. LinUCB requires initial training rounds to learn good paths. Real-time external retrieval adds latency and depends on web quality. Not yet evaluated on very long or multimodal documents.

title: Challenges for generative AI in legal reasoning
summary: Legal reasoning demands strict rules, hierarchies, and justifiable choices, yet LLMs often hallucinate citations or miss subtle distinctions. This review paper maps the biggest obstacles for using generative AI in judicial decision-making: picking the right law across jurisdictions, handling time and hierarchy, resolving ambiguity, managing conflicts, and applying burden of proof correctly. It evaluates current fixes like RAG and multi-agent systems and concludes that today's AI is useful only for simple tasks.
methodology: The authors use the IRAC (Issue–Rule–Application–Conclusion) framework to organize challenges. They examine real legal requirements—jurisdictional rules, stare decisis, transitional provisions, general clauses like 'reasonableness', conflicting norms, and evidence evaluation. For each challenge they discuss why LLMs struggle and test whether enhancements (RAG, multi-agent systems, neuro-symbolic AI) can help. No new experiments; the analysis draws on legal doctrine and recent AI case failures.
findings: LLMs frequently fail at selecting the correct legal framework (especially cross-border), respecting temporal scope of laws, distinguishing binding vs. persuasive precedent, and applying burden-of-proof rules. RAG improves factual recall but cannot resolve open-textured concepts or discretionary judgment. Multi-agent setups help break tasks but still lack transparent justification. Neuro-symbolic hybrids add logic but scale poorly for full case adjudication.
conclusion: Generative AI can already speed up routine legal work, but it cannot yet replace judges in complex cases requiring discretion and fully justifiable reasoning. A staged approach—use current tools for simple tasks today, invest in new methods for hierarchy, temporality, and evidence tomorrow—is the realistic path forward.
limitations: The paper is conceptual and does not run large-scale LLM benchmarks on real court cases. It focuses mainly on judicial (not contractual) reasoning. Practical performance of proposed enhancements (RAG, multi-agent) is discussed but not quantitatively tested in the legal domain.

title: Enhancing adversarial resilience in semantic caching for secure retrieval augmented generation systems
summary: Semantic caching speeds up RAG systems by reusing answers for similar questions, but attackers can trick it with tiny changes that create fake cache hits. This paper shows how GPTCache and similar industrial systems (Azure, Apigee) are vulnerable and introduces SAFE-CACHE, a new cluster-based method that groups similar questions together and checks against cluster centers instead of single entries.
methodology: SAFE-CACHE clusters historical Q&A pairs using Louvain community detection, removes noisy clusters with statistical purity/coherence checks, refines embeddings with a bi-encoder and adversarial triplet loss, and uses a fine-tuned 1B Gemma-3 LLM (LoRA) to enrich sparse clusters with intent-preserving paraphrases. At runtime, new queries are matched to cluster centroids. Adversarial attacks use MetMap transformations and GPT-4.1 semantic perturbations.
findings: SAFE-CACHE reduced successful adversarial attacks from 52.77% (GPTCache) to 14.27%—a 72% improvement—while keeping normal caching efficiency almost unchanged. It resisted embedding-collision, cache-poisoning, and flooding attacks much better. The lightweight LLM enrichment kept cluster quality high (98.3% schema adherence, 94.8% intent preservation).
conclusion: Cluster-centroid matching plus intent-aware enrichment makes semantic caching far more secure without sacrificing speed. SAFE-CACHE is a practical defense for real-world RAG deployments in enterprise chatbots and sensitive services.
limitations: Tested on two specific 300-intent datasets; broader real-world query distributions need more validation. The lightweight LLM adds slight overhead during cluster enrichment. Not yet evaluated against adaptive attackers who learn the defense over time.

title: CTI-Thinker: an LLM-driven system for CTI knowledge graph construction and attack reasoning
summary: Cyber threat intelligence reports are messy and hard to turn into usable knowledge. CTI-Thinker uses a fine-tuned LLaMA3-8B model plus in-context learning to extract entities and relations, aligns them to the ATT&CK framework, builds a clean knowledge graph, and then uses GraphRAG for smart attack-chain reasoning and question answering.
methodology: Step 1: ICL + LoRA fine-tuning extracts structured triples from CTI reports. Step 2: KNN retrieval and vector alignment fuse duplicate or paraphrased entities across documents. Step 3: GraphRAG combines the new CTI graph with external ATT&CK knowledge for tactical-level inference. The system supports interactive Q&A about APT tactics, techniques, and procedures.
findings: CTI-Thinker achieved higher precision and recall in entity/relation extraction than previous BERT-based or LLM methods. The resulting knowledge graph enabled accurate attack profiling and intent inference. GraphRAG reasoning outperformed plain LLM answers on complex APT scenarios, reducing hallucinations and improving explainability.
conclusion: By combining LLM extraction, semantic alignment, and graph-enhanced retrieval, CTI-Thinker turns raw threat reports into a reliable, reasoning-ready knowledge graph. It offers a scalable way to automate CTI analysis and support real-time cyber defense.
limitations: Depends on high-quality ATT&CK mappings; emerging or zero-day tactics may still be missed. Fine-tuning requires some domain-specific data. Performance on non-English CTI reports or very long multi-source documents was not fully tested. Real-time streaming threat data integration remains future work.

title: Addressing investor concerns: a Chinese financial question-answering benchmark with LLM-based evaluation
summary: AI is increasingly used in finance, but it struggles with Chinese financial documents because there aren't many high-quality, specialized datasets to train it on. This study introduces a new dataset called CFQA, built from real Chinese company annual reports, complete with human-checked questions and answers. The researchers found that current AI models perform poorly on this dataset because the original documents are complex PDFs with messy tables, the reports are extremely long, and the questions require deep, highly specific financial knowledge to answer correctly.
methodology: The researchers gathered public annual reports from Chinese listed companies and had humans write specific financial questions and correct answers for them. They then tested existing Large Language Models (LLMs) and smart search systems (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) against this new benchmark. They created an AI-based evaluator to automatically score how well the models could find the right information hidden in long, complex PDF formats and answer the tricky, domain-specific questions.
findings: The results showed that current, standard AI systems struggle significantly with the CFQA dataset. The complex tabular structures in the PDFs and the extreme length of the financial reports made it very hard for the AI to retrieve the correct information. The AI often failed to understand the highly specific financial terminology required to answer the questions accurately, leading to poor overall performance.
conclusion: The main takeaway is that general AI models are not yet ready to handle advanced Chinese financial analysis straight out of the box. The newly created CFQA benchmark provides a crucial, highly difficult testing ground that will push developers to create smarter, more specialized financial AI tools capable of reading messy, real-world corporate documents.
limitations: The main challenges involve the difficulty of cleanly extracting text and data from complex PDF tables. Additionally, because financial reports are so massive, the AI often loses track of the context. Finally, evaluating the AI's answers requires deep financial expertise, making it hard to fully automate the grading process without human oversight.

title: Agentic AI can help hospitals prepare for unprecedented weather
summary: As climate change gets worse, hospitals are facing extreme, unpredictable weather disasters that go completely beyond their standard emergency plans. Traditional planning, which relies on predicting specific disaster scenarios, often fails when multiple crises hit at once (a "perfect storm"). This paper suggests moving to a "threshold-based" framework, where hospitals prepare for when their critical operational limits are pushed, regardless of what the actual disaster is. It proposes using "Agentic AI"—smart computer programs that can take independent action—to help hospitals automatically manage these unprecedented crises.
methodology: The study proposes a conceptual shift from scenario-based planning to threshold-based planning. Instead of guessing exactly what weather will hit, the system defines the maximum stress a hospital's infrastructure (like power or bed capacity) can handle. The researchers explore how emerging Agentic AI systems can be programmed to constantly monitor these stress levels and automatically suggest or initiate emergency responses when those critical thresholds are breached during a sudden, unforeseen climate event.
findings: The paper argues that when "climatic black swans" (completely unexpected, severe weather events) occur, traditional emergency protocols quickly become useless. However, by utilizing a threshold-based framework powered by Agentic AI, hospitals can remain much more resilient. The AI can rapidly process massive amounts of unfolding data and adapt to situations that human planners never even imagined or prepared for.
conclusion: To survive the worsening impacts of climate change, healthcare systems must fundamentally change how they prepare for emergencies. Relying on past weather patterns is no longer safe. By adopting Agentic AI that focuses on system stress thresholds, hospitals can dynamically respond to unprecedented natural disasters, ensuring they remain fully operational when communities need them the most.
limitations: Since this is a forward-looking proposal, the main challenge is that Agentic AI systems are still emerging technologies and are not yet fully battle-tested in chaotic, real-world hospital environments. There are also significant technical and ethical hurdles in allowing an AI to make independent, life-or-death emergency decisions during a crisis.

title: Agentic publications: redesigning scientific publishing in the age of thinking large language models
summary: The number of scientific papers published every year is growing so fast that researchers cannot possibly read them all. This paper introduces the idea of an "Agentic Publication" to fix this. Instead of a traditional, static PDF, a research paper becomes a smart, interactive AI system. This means readers, or even other AI programs, can "talk" to the paper, ask it questions, and get customized explanations, completely changing how scientific knowledge is shared, verified, and understood in the modern age.
methodology: The researchers built a framework that combines the normal text and images of a paper with structured data, like knowledge graphs, using Large Language Models (LLMs). They use a method called Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combined with multiple AI "agents" that work together to verify facts. The system stores the paper's information in a special database so that users can ask questions and the AI can generate accurate, easy-to-read narrative answers directly from the paper's underlying data.
findings: A proof-of-concept demonstration proved that this new type of publication actually works. The system successfully allowed users to interact with the scientific data in multiple languages, provided different levels of detail depending on the user's needs, and allowed other computer programs to seamlessly access the data. It successfully synthesized new findings on the spot while maintaining strict scientific accuracy.
conclusion: The traditional format of publishing science is outdated. Transforming static research papers into interactive, AI-driven "Agentic Publications" allows scientists to easily navigate complex information and synthesize new ideas much faster. This approach bridges the gap between human readers and machine-readable data, making scientific knowledge far more accessible and dynamic.
limitations: The main challenges include ensuring the AI doesn't hallucinate or generate fake information when answering questions about the paper. It requires strict automated validation, expert human oversight, and clear ethical rules to maintain the integrity of the peer-review process and prevent the spread of AI-generated junk science.

title: A multi-module AI system for intelligent health insurance support using retrieval augmented generation
summary: Understanding health insurance policies is notoriously difficult for most people because of the complex legal jargon. This paper presents a new, all-in-one AI system designed to make health insurance easy to navigate. Instead of using separate tools for chatting, recommending policies, and searching documents, this system combines them all into one smart assistant. It uses a technique called Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to accurately search through massive policy documents and give users clear, personalized, and highly accurate insurance advice.
methodology: The researchers built a multi-part AI system. First, there is a chatbot for basic questions. Second, a recommendation engine translates complex policy documents into mathematical codes (embeddings) so it can quickly match policies to a user's specific needs. Third, a document search tool finds exact legal clauses in uploaded PDFs. Crucially, they added an "evaluator agent"—an internal AI judge that constantly grades the chatbot's answers for clarity, accuracy, and helpfulness, creating a feedback loop that trains the system to get better over time.
findings: The combined AI system performed incredibly well in tests. It scored very high on semantic retrieval tests (meaning it understood the deep meaning of the user's questions, not just basic keywords). It was perfectly accurate in recommending the top policies and highly effective at digging out specific legal clauses from dense documents. The addition of the "evaluator agent" successfully stopped the AI from making up false insurance rules.
conclusion: Dealing with health insurance doesn't have to be confusing. By combining an AI chatbot, a smart search engine, and a self-grading evaluation tool into one seamless system, companies can provide highly accurate, transparent, and user-friendly insurance support. This technology can significantly improve how people understand their coverage and make financial security more accessible to everyone.
limitations: The system requires converting highly complex, semi-structured documents (like PDFs with messy tables) into clean digital data, which is technically difficult. Furthermore, maintaining extreme accuracy is critical, as a single AI hallucination about a health insurance clause could have serious financial consequences for a user.

title: FusionITD: Enhanced Cross-Modal Insider Threat Perception Framework via Behavior-Semantic Fusion
summary: Insider threats — when employees misuse their access to harm an organization — are hard to detect because attackers already have legitimate access. Most existing systems only look at log data like file operations or login records, missing the rich meaning hidden in emails and browsing content. FusionITD is a new framework that combines two types of signals: behavioral patterns (what users do and when) and semantic content (what users write or browse). By fusing both, it builds a more complete picture of user intent, catching threats that either approach alone would miss. It was tested on real-world datasets and significantly outperformed existing methods.
methodology: FusionITD has three main parts. First, it builds a user behavior graph — a network connecting each user to the computers they interact with, organized by time windows (work hours vs. rest hours). A specially designed graph neural network called WR-GNN learns patterns from this graph. To handle the problem of very few abnormal samples among millions of normal ones, a modified loss function called Focal-MSE gives extra attention to rare anomalies during training. Second, for semantic analysis, it uses a technique called Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): when a user sends an email or visits a website, the system finds similar past behaviors from a knowledge base and asks a large language model to assess whether the current behavior shows negative emotions, suspicious intentions, or declining work focus. Third, an adaptive fusion mechanism using logistic regression dynamically combines the behavioral score and semantic score into a final threat prediction.
findings: On the CERT r4.2 and r5.2 datasets — standard benchmark datasets for insider threat research — FusionITD achieved AUC scores of 0.9765 and 0.9816, meaning it correctly separates normal from abnormal users almost 98% of the time. Its true positive rate reached 0.92–0.93, and its false positive rate dropped to just 0.01–0.04, far lower than competing methods like LAN, DeepLog, and LogPrompt. Ablation studies confirmed that each component — the graph module, the Focal-MSE loss, and the semantic module — meaningfully contributes to performance. Adding semantic dimensions one at a time (intention, then emotion, then focus) progressively improved results.
conclusion: FusionITD demonstrates that combining structured behavioral data with unstructured semantic content is a powerful strategy for insider threat detection. Its design — using graph neural networks for temporal behavior modeling and RAG-augmented language models for semantic understanding — is both interpretable and computationally practical. The adaptive fusion ensures that both modalities are weighted appropriately for different threat scenarios. This framework meets enterprise-level requirements for accuracy and low false alarms, making it a strong candidate for real-world deployment in organizational security systems.
limitations: The framework relies on a manually labeled semantic knowledge base, which requires significant human effort to build and maintain. Privacy concerns arise when large language models analyze sensitive employee communications. The system's performance depends on sufficient historical data to build behavioral baselines. In resource-limited settings, the full parallel RAG-LLM analysis may be too slow, requiring a cascaded approach that could miss some threats. Future work mentions integrating federated learning or trusted execution environments to protect user data privacy during analysis.

title: GastroTCM: A Large Language Model Assistant for Gastroenterology in Traditional Chinese Medicine
summary: Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) is widely used for digestive problems like gastritis, but it's complex and hard for AI systems to understand because it uses unique concepts like 'syndrome differentiation' and herbal formulas not found in standard medical databases. General-purpose AI models hallucinate (make things up) and lack TCM-specific knowledge. GastroTCM is a specialized AI assistant built on the Llama3-8B model, fine-tuned on real clinical records and enhanced with a knowledge retrieval system. It can engage in meaningful multi-turn conversations with patients, ask follow-up questions, and provide accurate TCM-based advice for gastrointestinal conditions — outperforming strong Chinese language models in evaluations.
methodology: GastroTCM was built in several steps. First, about 20 million tokens of de-identified clinical records from real TCM gastroenterology consultations were collected and formatted as realistic multi-turn dialogues (intake, questioning, diagnosis, recommendation). An 80/10/10 train/validation/test split was used, with near-duplicate detection to prevent data leakage. The model was fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), which efficiently adjusts model weights without full retraining, using a single RTX 4090 GPU. Second, a vector knowledge database was built from TCM clinical terminology standards and 1,002 research papers, chunked into segments of about 2,000 tokens with overlap, and embedded as 512-dimensional vectors indexed by symptoms and keywords. During inference, an intelligent agent filters irrelevant retrieved content using cosine similarity and an information entropy value check, ensuring only relevant knowledge augments the model's response.
findings: In single-turn question answering evaluated using BLEU scores against expert-defined reference answers, GastroTCM with the RAG agent scored 0.334 on average, notably higher than ChatGLM-6B at 0.218 and Qwen-2 at 0.246. In multi-turn dialogue evaluation, GastroTCM proactively asked follow-up questions (a sign of clinical reasoning) in 45% of cases, compared to just 1.7% for ChatGLM and 3.3% for Qwen. Expert TCM physicians confirmed that GastroTCM provided more accurate, safer, and more professional responses. The RAG module specifically reduced hallucinated or unsupported statements, with an expert-verified win rate of about 68% against baselines.
conclusion: GastroTCM shows that domain-specific fine-tuning combined with retrieval-augmented generation can create a genuinely useful clinical assistant for a specialized and culturally unique medical system like TCM. Rather than replacing doctors, it acts as an intelligent support tool that improves patient understanding, aids doctor-patient communication, and could expand access to quality TCM guidance in resource-limited areas. It demonstrates a practical and reproducible pipeline for building specialized medical LLMs even with limited computational resources, using LoRA on a single consumer GPU.
limitations: The training data comes from a single institution and cannot be publicly released due to privacy regulations, limiting reproducibility. The Llama3 backbone has lower inherent Chinese language understanding compared to Chinese-first models like Qwen or ChatGLM, which constrains performance in purely linguistic tasks. The evaluation was conducted on only 20 benchmark cases, which may not capture the full diversity of real clinical scenarios. Future work should expand to multimodal inputs like tongue coating images, improve cross-disease generalization, and conduct real-world clinical trials to validate safety and effectiveness.

title: Hint Recognition in Chinese and Russian Diplomatic Discourse Using Large Language Models
summary: In diplomacy, what is left unsaid often matters more than what is stated directly. Hints — implicit messages embedded in polite or indirect language — are routinely used by diplomats but are easily misunderstood, even causing historical crises. This study builds an AI system to automatically detect and interpret hints in real press conference transcripts from the Chinese and Russian foreign ministries. It combines a linguistic theory of hints (covering their verbal, cultural, and strategic dimensions) with a retrieval-based knowledge system and structured step-by-step reasoning. The system achieved strong overall performance, with Russian hints being easier to detect than Chinese ones, and revealed three systematic error types that point toward concrete improvements.
methodology: The researchers first manually analyzed 1,389 diplomatic dialogue segments to identify and classify 498 hints into three types: Fixed Hints (stable forms with predictable meanings), Cultural Hints (stable forms whose meaning requires cultural knowledge), and Contextual Hints (flexible forms where meaning depends entirely on the conversational situation). Fixed and Cultural Hints were vectorized and stored in a searchable knowledge base. For recognizing new hints, the system uses a two-step process: first, it retrieves semantically similar known hints from the knowledge base using cosine similarity (the RAG step). If no match is found, it applies Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning — a structured prompting method that walks the model through checking pragmatic motivations (criticism, refutation, warning, etc.) and strategies (normative statement, factual statement, irony, etc.) before deciding whether a hint is present. A self-evaluation step then checks the output for consistency before finalizing it.
findings: Tested on 100 new diplomatic exchanges each from Chinese and Russian 2025 transcripts, the system achieved an overall F1 score of 0.829 for Russian and 0.757 for Chinese. Both corpora showed high recall (the system rarely missed real hints), but precision was lower for Chinese, meaning it sometimes labeled non-hints as hints. The largest performance gap between the two languages occurred in Cultural Hints (F1 of 0.857 for Russian vs. 0.588 for Chinese), because Chinese cultural hints rely on context-dependent idioms and diplomatic formulas that are harder for the AI to match. Three error types were identified: semantic over-interpretation (inventing meanings), hint-type misclassification (getting the category wrong), and literal misclassification (treating direct statements as hints).
conclusion: This study provides a theoretically grounded and empirically tested pathway for using AI to decode subtle implicit communication in high-stakes diplomatic language. The combination of RAG (for stable hint types) and CoT reasoning (for context-dependent ones) proved effective and mutually complementary. The error analysis generates actionable improvements: expanding negative-example training data, anchoring pragmatic inferences to actual discourse context, adding a repeated-matching mechanism, calibrating trigger words, and inserting a pre-filtering step before classification. This work has broader implications for multilingual implicit information extraction beyond diplomacy.
limitations: Hint identification inherently involves subjectivity, and despite inter-annotator agreement testing (Cohen's Kappa of 0.74 for Chinese and 0.69 for Russian), disagreements exist. The study only compares two languages — Chinese and Russian — which share some geographic and cognitive proximity, potentially limiting generalizability. The RAG knowledge base requires manual curation and may not cover all variant forms of fixed expressions, causing mismatches. Future work should extend to agglutinative and polysynthetic languages, include corpora from more culturally diverse countries, and increase the number of expert annotators for more robust gold-standard annotations.

title: Implementing Generative Artificial Intelligence in Precision Oncology: Safety, Governance, and Significance
summary: Precision oncology tailors cancer treatment to each patient's unique tumor genetics and clinical history, but translating complex genomic data into clinical decisions remains slow, costly, and inconsistent. Generative AI — including large language models and vision-language models — has matured enough to serve as a bridge between scientific literature, clinical guidelines, and patient data. This comprehensive review surveys how generative AI can support three key oncology tasks: interpreting genetic mutations, matching patients to clinical trials, and extracting tumor characteristics from imaging and pathology images. It also proposes a responsible implementation roadmap centered on retrieval-augmented generation, human oversight, data standardization, and regulatory compliance to move toward a continuously learning oncology ecosystem.
methodology: This is a narrative review synthesizing literature from PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, FDA device clearances, and official company documents up to December 2025. The review traces generative AI's evolution from early Restricted Boltzmann Machines through GANs, Variational Autoencoders, and Transformers (including Mixture-of-Experts architectures) to modern multimodal foundation models. It then organizes clinical applications along three axes: (1) LLM-based mutation interpretation using RAG to cross-reference databases like OncoKB and CIViC; (2) trial eligibility matching frameworks like TrialGPT, which retrieves, filters, and ranks clinical trials against patient EHR profiles using zero-shot LLM reasoning; and (3) multimodal foundation models (e.g., Prov-GigaPath for pathology, Flamingo-CXR for radiology) that derive tumor phenotypes from images. Implementation strategies focus on RAG for knowledge freshness, Human-in-the-Loop workflows for safety, data harmonization via OMOP/mCODE/FHIR standards, differential privacy for synthetic data, and governance aligned with the EU AI Act and Good Machine Learning Practice.
findings: Across reviewed studies, LLMs showed strong potential in mutation classification (GPT-4o achieved 73% accuracy on cancer variant classification, outperforming Llama and Qwen), with RAG and prompt engineering further improving performance. TrialGPT recalled over 90% of relevant trials while reviewing fewer than 6% of all candidates, and reduced clinician matching time by about 43%. Radiology report generation AI (like Flamingo-CXR) produced reports rated equivalent to or better than human reports in 77% of normal-finding cases. Ambient AI documentation tools reduced physician note-writing time and burnout in multiple observational studies. However, hallucinations, outdated knowledge, and performance inconsistency across institutions remain persistent challenges.
conclusion: Generative AI holds genuine transformative potential for precision oncology by accelerating mutation interpretation, expanding trial access, and improving diagnostic reporting. However, deployment must follow a 'safety-by-design' philosophy rather than a speed-to-deployment mindset. The review proposes 'learning oncology' as the ultimate goal — a continuously improving system where AI outputs are grounded by RAG, reviewed by clinicians, validated prospectively across multiple centers, and governed through auditable logs and accountability frameworks. This iterative cycle, combining AI capability with human oversight, can progressively improve patient outcomes while maintaining safety and fairness.
limitations: Current LLMs frequently hallucinate or use outdated knowledge, which is dangerous in rapidly evolving fields like oncology where drug approvals and guidelines change frequently. Performance often degrades across different hospitals due to variations in EHR structure, imaging protocols, and local documentation practices. Bias exists in training data, potentially exacerbating existing disparities in cancer treatment access. Synthetic EHR data, while privacy-preserving, may underrepresent rare tumor types. Regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act impose significant compliance burdens. Most clinical evaluations are single-center or observational; large randomized controlled trials validating AI impact on patient outcomes are still limited.

title: RAGMail: a cloud-based retrieval-augmented framework for reducing hallucinations in LLM text generation
summary: Cold emails are vital for job seekers but hard to personalize at scale. RAGMail is a cloud-native system that uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) plus Factualness Evaluation via Weighting LLMs (FEWL) to generate accurate, personalized cold emails. It pulls real job descriptions and resume details, grounds the LLM output in retrieved facts, and applies cloud-hosted templates and monitoring for scalability and privacy.
methodology: The system extracts job postings and candidate resumes, converts them into vector embeddings, and stores them in a scalable cloud vector database. For each cold-email request, RAG retrieves the most relevant context. A lightweight pre-deployment metamodel conditions retrieval and generation on cloud constraints (latency, cost, SLA). FEWL assigns weights to key facts (skills, experience, relevance) and verifies output consistency before final generation.
findings: RAGMail produces emails with significantly higher factual accuracy and recruiter engagement than generic LLM outputs. Human evaluations confirm reduced hallucinations, strong personalization, and improved response rates. The cloud architecture delivers elastic scaling, low latency, and real-time monitoring while protecting data privacy.
conclusion: RAGMail demonstrates that combining RAG with factual weighting and cloud-native design creates a practical, hallucination-resistant tool for high-volume personalized outreach. The framework extends beyond job emails to sales, academia, and other domains where factual grounding and context sensitivity matter.
limitations: Performance depends on the quality and freshness of retrieved job/resume data. Tested primarily on English-language job applications; multilingual support and very niche industries need further validation. Real-time external retrieval adds minor latency during peak usage.

title: SKiM-GPT: combining biomedical literature-based discovery with large language model hypothesis evaluation
summary: Literature-based discovery tools like SKiM find statistical links between biomedical terms but leave researchers to manually interpret thousands of results. SKiM-GPT adds an LLM-powered RAG layer that automatically evaluates user-defined hypotheses using the most relevant PubMed abstracts, returning transparent scores and grounded justifications.
methodology: SKiM first identifies statistically significant A-B-C co-occurrence links and retrieves the associated abstracts. A fine-tuned Phi-3 relevance filter keeps only abstracts pertinent to the hypothesis. The filtered abstracts plus a structured scoring prompt are sent to a frontier LLM, which assigns a –2 to +2 support score and explains its reasoning with direct citations.
findings: On 14 disease-gene-drug hypotheses, SKiM-GPT achieved strong ordinal agreement with four expert biologists (Cohen’s κ = 0.84). Relevance filtering boosted directional concordance to 100% and kept median scores within human ranges in 86% of cases. The system is fully transparent: every score is accompanied by the exact abstracts and natural-language rationale used.
conclusion: SKiM-GPT bridges the gap between statistical discovery and biological interpretation. By combining co-occurrence search with grounded LLM evaluation, it dramatically reduces manual curation effort while maintaining expert-level reliability. The open-source tool and web interface make hypothesis evaluation scalable for wet-lab and computational researchers alike.
limitations: Relies on PubMed abstracts; very recent or non-indexed literature is not covered. Performance depends on the quality of the relevance filter and the chosen frontier LLM. Not yet tested on extremely rare diseases or multi-hop hypotheses spanning dozens of papers.

title: Retrieval-augmented patch generation for geosynchronous satellite status forecasting
summary: Geosynchronous satellite data is non-stationary and weakly periodic, making accurate status forecasting and intent classification difficult. RAPG solves this with adaptive frequency-domain patching plus retrieval of historically similar patches, allowing a Transformer to capture both short-term dynamics and long-range patterns.
methodology: Each satellite time series is transformed via Discrete Fourier Transform to identify dominant frequencies and compute adaptive patch lengths. Patches are embedded and fed to a Transformer. A retrieval block then finds the top-k most similar historical key patches and aggregates their future value patches to augment the current input. The model jointly performs status forecasting (MSE loss) and intent classification.
findings: On synthetic and real GEO datasets, RAPG achieved the lowest forecasting errors and an F1-score of 0.9461 / accuracy of 0.9443 for intent classification—outperforming strong baselines. The combination of frequency-aware patching and historical retrieval proved especially effective for non-stationary, maneuver-heavy sequences.
conclusion: RAPG provides a robust, scalable framework for real-time GEO satellite analysis. By explicitly linking adaptive temporal patching with retrieval-based memory, it overcomes the limitations of standard deep models on weakly periodic orbital data and offers practical value for space situational awareness.
limitations: Tested on specific synthetic and real GEO datasets; broader orbital regimes (LEO, MEO) need validation. Retrieval depends on a well-curated historical patch database. Computational cost of frequency analysis and retrieval adds slight overhead during inference.

title: Simulated patient systems powered by large language model-based AI agents offer potential for transforming medical education
summary: Traditional human-simulated patients are expensive and hard to scale. AIPatient uses six LLM-based agents, Reasoning RAG, and a knowledge graph built from real MIMIC-III records to create realistic, consistent, and verifiable virtual patients for medical training.
methodology: Patient profiles are constructed from de-identified EHRs using NER and stored in a Neo4j knowledge graph. A multi-agent Reasoning RAG workflow (Retrieval, Abstraction, Checker, Rewrite, Summarization) processes user queries, retrieves relevant facts, reasons step-by-step to reduce hallucinations, and generates empathetic first-person responses conditioned on personality profiles.
findings: AIPatient achieved 94.15% QA accuracy with all agents enabled, high knowledge validity (F1=0.89), excellent readability (median Flesch Grade 6.4), and strong robustness/stability across paraphrased questions and 32 personalities. Medical students rated it comparable or superior to human-simulated patients in fidelity, usability, and educational value.
conclusion: AIPatient demonstrates that LLM-agent systems with grounded knowledge and structured reasoning can deliver accurate, readable, and trustworthy simulated patients at scale. It has strong potential to make medical education more accessible, consistent, and cost-effective.
limitations: Built on MIMIC-III data (intensive-care focus); performance on outpatient or rare conditions needs broader validation. Relies on cloud APIs for some LLMs, raising cost and latency considerations for very large deployments. Personality simulation, while effective, is still text-based and lacks non-verbal cues.

title: KT-LLM: an evidence-grounded and sequence text framework for auditable kidney transplant modeling
summary: Kidney transplant care suffers from fragmented data, inconsistent policy adherence, and non-reproducible outcomes across centers. KT-LLM is a verifiable orchestration layer that tightly couples structured longitudinal follow-up (OPTN/SRTR TRF sequences) with versioned textual rules (Banff pathology, OPTN policies, SRTR reporting cadence) via retrieval-augmented generation and three auditable agents. The system delivers personalized survival predictions, clinically distinct Black-recipient subtypes, and policy-compliant checklists with full provenance.
methodology: A scoped RAG layer restricts knowledge to versioned Banff Central Repository, OPTN/UNOS policies, and SRTR methodological notes. An LLM orchestrator parses queries and dispatches three agents: Agent-A (SRTR-MambaSurv) uses a linear-time Mamba backbone for discrete-time survival and competing-risks modeling on TRF-aligned trajectories; Agent-B (OPTN-BlackClust) performs deep embedded clustering to discover stable Black-recipient subtypes; Agent-C (Policy-Ops) encodes submission deadlines, PSR cadence, and Banff terminology into executable rules returning pass/warn/fail verdicts with citations. Outputs are aligned, unit-checked, and aggregated into structured answers with evidence pointers and confidence gating.
findings: On de-identified OPTN/UNOS cohorts (2015–2019), KT-LLM outperformed strong baselines in evidence attribution, predictive calibration (C-index, time-dependent AUC, integrated Brier), clustering stability (ARI/NMI, bootstrap Jaccard), and policy concordance. It successfully surfaced clinically distinct Black-recipient subgroups while maintaining auditability and avoiding overgeneralization beyond the analyzed window. Citation hit rate and structured checklist consistency were markedly higher than closed-book or generic medical LLMs.
conclusion: By anchoring reasoning to versioned policies, TRF sequences, and Banff terminology, KT-LLM transforms kidney-transplant modeling into an auditable, clock-synchronized process. It offers a practical path toward reproducible, equitable, and policy-compliant KT care that can scale across centers and eras.
limitations: Evaluated on 2015–2019 OPTN/UNOS data; performance on newer cohorts or non-US registries requires re-validation. Survival modeling truncates at PSR freeze dates, potentially limiting long-term horizon accuracy. Real-time integration with live EHR systems and multi-modal imaging (e.g., biopsy slides) was not tested.

title: Prompt engineering as cognitive scaffolding for ethical and explanatory quality in AI-mediated financial learning
summary: Large language models are increasingly used for personal-finance education, yet prompt phrasing strongly influences the pedagogical quality of generated content. This Design-Based Research (DBR) Phase 1 study validates a prompt taxonomy that treats prompting as instructional design, systematically improving financial accuracy, conceptual clarity, and ethical tone.
methodology: A 120-prompt corpus was generated with GPT-4-turbo (May 2025). Phase 1a (n=90) used a factorial design varying linguistic framing (declarative/procedural/interrogative), cognitive complexity (Bloom’s levels), ethical anchoring, and finance topics. Phase 1b (n=30) instantiated a derived taxonomy of three design principles: Guided Explanation, Contextualised Inquiry, and Comparative Reasoning. Two expert raters scored outputs (0–3) on accuracy, clarity, and ethical tone (inter-rater r_s = 0.82). Non-parametric tests and tail-risk analysis compared conditions.
findings: Procedural prompts outperformed interrogatives on clarity (U=418, p<.001, r=.42) and accuracy (U=457, p<.01, r=.31). Explicit ethical anchoring raised ethical tone (median 2.78 vs 2.31; Cliff’s δ=.46, p<.001) with only a modest accuracy trade-off. Taxonomy-guided prompts increased medians for clarity (+0.46 to 2.92), accuracy (+0.41 to 2.81), and ethical tone (+0.37 to 2.71), while reducing low-quality outliers from 18% to 4% (χ²=7.91, p=.005).
conclusion: Prompt engineering functions as cognitive scaffolding. The validated taxonomy operationalizes three evidence-based design principles that reliably elevate instructional quality in high-stakes financial learning. These principles link prompt form to pedagogical function and provide actionable guidance for educators and AI-content designers.
limitations: Phase 1 evaluates content quality only (no learner outcomes). Tested exclusively on GPT-4-turbo (May 2025); cross-model robustness requires Phase 2 validation. Finance topics were limited to budgeting, credit, debt, savings, loans, and insurance; broader domains (tax, retirement) were not covered.

title: LLM-driven collaborative framework for knowledge-enhanced cancer pain assessment and management
summary: Cancer pain is multi-factorial, opioid-response variable, and high-risk. OncoPainBot is a multi-agent LLM framework that simulates a multidisciplinary team through four specialized agents (Pain-Extraction, Pain-Mechanism Reasoning, Treatment-Planning, Safety-Check). It integrates NCCN/CSCO/WHO guidelines via RAG to deliver personalized, evidence-based, and auditable analgesic recommendations.
methodology: Four LLMs (Claude 4, ChatGPT 4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GLM 4.5) were benchmarked on MedMCQA, PubMedQA, and a cancer-specific subset. Hybrid RAG (semantic + BM25) was selected for optimal accuracy–efficiency balance. The four agents process 516 retrospective cancer-pain EMRs: structured extraction → mechanism reasoning → treatment planning → safety verification. Outputs were evaluated for semantic alignment (BERTScore, ROUGE, BLEU) and clinical decision accuracy against ground-truth prescriptions.
findings: Claude 4 + Hybrid RAG achieved the highest accuracy (MedMCQA-Cancer >80%) and strongest semantic alignment with real clinical notes (top BERTScore, ROUGE-L/2/1). Analgesic recommendation accuracy reached 0.841 overall; most errors were patient-specific factors or monitoring rather than drug-class mistakes. OncoPainBot consistently outperformed single-LLM and non-RAG baselines while maintaining acceptable latency for clinical use.
conclusion: OncoPainBot demonstrates that a knowledge-grounded, multi-agent LLM framework can deliver transparent, evidence-based, and clinically aligned cancer-pain management. It offers a scalable, auditable assistant that augments rather than replaces multidisciplinary care.
limitations: Retrospective EMR validation only; prospective real-time deployment and clinician-in-the-loop studies are needed. Knowledge base limited to NCCN/CSCO/WHO guidelines; institution-specific protocols or latest trial data require dynamic updates. Safety-check performance on rare drug–drug interactions or organ-dysfunction edge cases needs further stress testing.

title: Nalvakku: fact-based counter narratives for Tamil hate speech
summary: Tamil hate speech (misogyny, xenophobia) lacks scalable, culturally grounded counter-narratives. Nalvakku expands a 220-pair seed dataset to 5,000 high-quality HS–CN pairs via human-in-the-loop Author–Reviewer validation, then generates fact-based responses using external knowledge retrieval and a final Fact-RAG system that incorporates the human-validated corpus as a curated knowledge base.
methodology: Stage 1: Human-in-the-loop expansion of misogynistic/xenophobic HS–CN pairs using iterative LLM generation and expert review. Stage 2: Fact-based generation retrieves and summarizes external sources (Wikipedia, trusted domains) to ground counter-narratives. Stage 3: Integrated Fact-RAG combines the 5,000 human-post-edited pairs with retrieved facts for final generation. Multiple LLMs were benchmarked; evaluation used intrinsic metrics and LLM-as-judge assessments of diversity, credibility, and relevance.
findings: The human-in-the-loop pipeline produced linguistically accurate and culturally appropriate Tamil counter-narratives. Fact-based RAG significantly improved factual grounding and persuasiveness compared with standard prompting. The final Fact-RAG system, leveraging the expert-curated knowledge base, generated varied, credible, and contextually relevant responses while reducing hallucinations and generic content.
conclusion: Nalvakku demonstrates that combining human supervision, external factual retrieval, and a validated knowledge base enables high-quality, low-resource counter-narrative generation in Tamil. The approach offers a practical, scalable model for combating online hate while preserving free expression and cultural nuance.
limitations: Focused on misogyny and xenophobia; other hate categories (e.g., caste, religion) require extension. Evaluation is largely automatic/LLM-based; large-scale human user studies on real social-media impact are pending. Tamil-specific dialectal and code-mixed variations need broader coverage.

title: Reliability-by-Design for Agentic GenAI: Turning the AI Risk Atlas Into an EU AI Act-Ready Assurance Case
summary: This paper tackles a big shift in AI: systems are no longer just giving advice — they are now taking actions on their own. These 'agentic' AI systems can invoke tools, trigger workflows, and make decisions without human approval at every step. This creates new reliability challenges because mistakes can cascade into real-world harm. The paper connects IBM's AI Risk Atlas with the EU AI Act to propose a practical engineering framework that makes agentic AI systems dependable, traceable, and governable — not just compliant on paper.
methodology: The author builds a 'reliability-by-design' framework by first categorizing agentic AI risks into four groups: explainability, privacy, fairness, and robustness. Each risk is mapped to a specific failure mode, a control mechanism (like confidence thresholds or context redaction), and the evidence needed to prove it's working. The paper then proposes action-oriented reliability metrics — such as 'unsafe action attempt rate' and 'intervention rate' — to replace traditional accuracy scores. Finally, it outlines a five-part EU AI Act-ready assurance case covering lifecycle assurance, structured arguments, accountability, operational boundaries, and continuous monitoring.
findings: Traditional metrics like accuracy are insufficient for acting AI systems. The paper proposes new metrics including time to anomaly detection, trace completeness, blocked action ratio, and cross-tool propagation depth. These reveal behavioral drift, overconfidence, and governance gaps that accuracy scores miss. The EU AI Act's requirements for traceability, human oversight, and post-market monitoring directly align with reliability engineering principles — making regulatory compliance and engineering best practice mutually reinforcing rather than separate concerns.
conclusion: Agentic GenAI demands a fundamental rethinking of reliability engineering. The paper argues that assurance must extend beyond model correctness to cover the entire decision-action loop. By treating the EU AI Act as a reliability framework rather than a legal burden, organizations can build autonomous systems that are auditable and defensible. The assurance case proposed becomes a living engineering asset — not a one-time checklist — evolving as the system updates and expands its operational scope.
limitations: The framework is conceptual and lacks empirical validation in real production deployments. It relies heavily on the IBM AI Risk Atlas taxonomy, which may not cover all emerging agentic risk patterns. Implementation complexity — especially for mid-sized organizations — is not addressed. The paper also does not fully explore multi-agent or hierarchical agentic systems, where failure propagation is even more complex and harder to trace.

title: The Rise of Agentic AI in Finance: Opportunities, Risks, and Human-Centric Integration
summary: This paper examines how agentic AI — AI that can perceive, reason, and act autonomously — is transforming financial services. Unlike basic chatbots or rule-based tools, agentic AI can execute trades, detect fraud, personalize financial advice, and handle regulatory compliance with minimal human input. Major firms like Deloitte, EY, PwC, and Mastercard are already deploying such systems. The paper also honestly examines the serious risks these systems bring, including data security threats, regulatory challenges, high infrastructure costs, and the danger of flawed data feeding automated decisions at scale.
methodology: The paper uses a survey and case-study approach, drawing on industry reports, recent deployments, and academic sources. It categorizes agentic AI's financial applications into internal efficiency (fraud detection, compliance, data processing) and customer-facing services (personalized advice, proactive financial planning). It then analyzes real-world implementations — particularly PwC's Agent OS and Schedule K-1 processing — to illustrate both the gains (two-week tasks reduced to one day) and the human-in-the-loop (HITL) design principles needed to keep humans in control while automation scales.
findings: Banks using agentic AI report 20–30% productivity gains. Mastercard's fraud detection system doubled card compromise detection and cut false positives by 200%. PwC's hybrid AI-human tax teams slashed K-1 production from nearly two weeks to one day. The EU AI Act classifies credit-scoring agentic systems as high-risk, requiring strict risk mitigation before deployment. A key finding is that data quality — not model sophistication — is often the binding constraint: flawed data fed to autonomous agents causes mistakes that scale rapidly.
conclusion: Agentic AI is moving beyond experimentation to become a genuine operational force in finance. However, its full potential can only be realized responsibly through HITL oversight models, strong data governance, role-based access controls, and alignment with regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act. Financial institutions must strategically identify high-impact use cases, invest in AI literacy for their teams, and build robust privacy and security frameworks before scaling agentic deployments.
limitations: The paper is largely descriptive and lacks quantitative benchmarking across firms. It does not deeply analyze failure cases or near-misses in agentic finance deployments. ROI measurement remains opaque due to vendor-subsidized early adoption phases. The discussion of regulatory compliance is primarily EU-centric and may not generalize to other jurisdictions. Long-term risks of over-reliance on agentic systems — particularly in market stress scenarios — are acknowledged but not thoroughly analyzed.

title: Toward Edge General Intelligence With Agentic AI and Agentification: Concepts, Technologies, and Future Directions
summary: This comprehensive survey addresses a critical gap: how to bring powerful, autonomous AI agents to resource-constrained edge devices in 6G networks. Traditional edge AI uses static, task-specific models that can't adapt. Agentic AI — which perceives, reasons, and acts in continuous loops — can transform edge nodes from passive relays into intelligent, self-directing agents. The paper surveys the full landscape: foundational concepts, enabling technologies like model compression and RAG, open-source frameworks, real case studies in UAV networks and vehicular computing, and promising future research directions for trustworthy deployment.
methodology: The survey organizes agentic AI for edge general intelligence around four design pillars: Compactness (lightweight models via LoRA, quantization, pruning, distillation), Efficiency (real-time inference through early-exit and task offloading), Knowledge and Reasoning (RAG, chain-of-thought, long-term memory), and Migration (knowledge transfer across changing environments). Four detailed case studies demonstrate these principles in practice: UAV-assisted IoT data collection using LLM-designed reward functions, intent-driven networking with contextual RAG retrieval, vehicular edge computing with semantic communication and GAE-PPO, and human-centric service provisioning via preference-guided deep reinforcement learning.
findings: LLM-enhanced reinforcement learning agents consistently outperform manually designed counterparts. In UAV networking, the Agentic TD3 agent achieved a 6.4% reduction in energy consumption versus its pure DRL equivalent. In intent networking, the Agentic Contextual Retrieval framework improved task success by 14.8% and reduced communication overhead by 23.4% compared to keyword-based methods. In vehicular edge computing, the GAE-PPO agentic approach outperformed vanilla PPO by approximately 61% in accumulated return. Human-centric service provisioning showed a 27.3% improvement in perceived quality of experience over uniform-preference DRL baselines.
conclusion: Agentic AI represents a fundamental evolution from static edge intelligence to adaptive, goal-driven systems capable of operating autonomously within tight resource constraints. The agentification process — converting passive devices into autonomous agents — is the key paradigm shift enabling edge general intelligence. Success requires co-designing compactness, efficiency, reasoning, and migration capabilities together rather than in isolation. The survey identifies regulation-aware, ethically aligned agentic intelligence and edge-cloud collaborative architectures as critical frontier research areas for safe, scalable deployment.
limitations: The survey acknowledges that deploying full agentic stacks on severely resource-constrained devices remains unsolved — especially for long-horizon reasoning tasks. Safety and formal verification of autonomous edge agents in safety-critical applications (healthcare, autonomous driving) lack mature frameworks. Privacy-preserving federated learning for multi-agent edge systems is still in early stages. Cross-domain adaptation without catastrophic forgetting under tight memory budgets remains a major open challenge. Ethical alignment and regulatory compliance mechanisms are identified as future work rather than solved problems.

title: NICT/ATR Chinese-Japanese-English Speech-to-Speech Translation System
summary: This paper describes a handheld device built by NICT/ATR that can translate speech in real time between Chinese, Japanese, and English. The whole system fits in one small terminal, so travelers can use it anywhere without needing an internet connection to a server. The key idea is that everything — listening to speech, converting it to text, translating it, and speaking the result — happens on the device itself. The system was trained on large collections of travel-related sentences, making it robust and flexible across different topics and accents.
methodology: The system has three main parts working together. The speech recognition part uses noise-suppression techniques — including a math tool called MMSE with Gaussian mixture models and a particle filter — to clean up the audio before recognizing it. It was trained on thousands of speakers across multiple accents. The machine translation part uses two modules: one that directly matches sentences to known translations, and another that breaks sentences into phrases and finds the best statistical translation using probabilities. Chinese text is split into meaningful word chunks using a three-step process involving dictionary lookup and a tagging model. The speech synthesis part, called XIMERA, uses large recorded speech databases and statistical models to generate natural-sounding output audio.
findings: For Chinese speech recognition, the system achieved character accuracy between 82% and 94% depending on the speaking style and noise level. The rejection of unreliable speech outputs kept utterance correctness above 83% across all test conditions. For translation quality, the BLEU scores ranged from 0.55 to 0.75, with Japanese-English pairs performing best. Human evaluators rated 78–86% of Japanese-English translations as perfect or good, while Chinese-English translations were somewhat harder, with about 52% rated perfect and 20% rated nonsense.
conclusion: The corpus-based approach across all three components — recognition, translation, and synthesis — proved effective for building a practical multilingual translation device. By putting everything into one handheld terminal, the system achieves real-time, location-independent translation for travelers. The modular design also makes it easy to add more language pairs in the future. The system represents a significant step toward breaking language barriers in everyday communication situations.
limitations: The system's recognition accuracy drops for natural conversational speech compared to read speech, especially in noisy environments like airports. The vocabulary is limited to travel-related topics, so it may struggle with technical or domain-specific conversations. The translation quality for Chinese-English pairs is noticeably weaker than for Japanese-English. The device size and processing power also limit how much more complex the models can get without hardware upgrades.

title: Severity-Controllable Pathological Text-to-Speech Synthesis for Clinical Applications
summary: This paper introduces a text-to-speech system that can generate speech sounding like someone with a speech disorder — and crucially, it can control how severe that disorder sounds. The motivation is clinical: patients about to undergo oral cancer surgery often want to understand how their voice might change afterward. Instead of using inconsistent recordings from different patients, this system uses a single consistent synthetic voice that can demonstrate speech across multiple severity levels. This helps patients mentally prepare for post-surgery voice changes in a standardized, controllable way.
methodology: The approach has four steps. First, a voice conversion model is trained to transfer the speaking characteristics (severity) of oral cancer patients onto a single healthy speaker's voice, creating a dataset where one speaker sounds progressively more impaired. Second, this converted dataset is used to train a text-to-speech model. Third, the model uses 'x-vectors' — compact numerical representations of a speaker's voice characteristics — as a control knob. Fourth, at synthesis time, the system blends the x-vector of a healthy speaker with that of a severely impaired speaker using a simple weighted average formula, smoothly controlling how severe the output sounds. The proposed GradTTS model uses a diffusion-based architecture with an explicit duration predictor to better handle the slow, irregular timing typical of pathological speech.
findings: GradTTS consistently outperformed the baseline TransformerTTS system across all measures. It produced more stable speech durations that better matched real pathological speech patterns (lower RMSE). Its severity control was more linear and predictable, with a correlation of 0.99 between the control variable and perceived severity. Naturalness scores were consistently higher for GradTTS. Phoneme error rates were lower across all severity levels, meaning the speech was more intelligible. Speaker similarity tests confirmed both systems produce single-speaker multi-severity speech rather than impersonating multiple speakers.
conclusion: The paper successfully demonstrates that a text-to-speech system can synthesize pathological speech with controllable severity using only a small amount of real pathological data. The key insight is that x-vectors — originally designed for speaker identity — also encode disease severity information, which can be leveraged without needing explicit severity labels. The GradTTS diffusion-based architecture handles the timing variability of disordered speech much better than autoregressive models. This system provides a practical foundation for clinical tools that help patients understand their likely post-surgery voice.
limitations: The system currently generates only one synthetic voice, not the patient's own voice, which limits personalization. X-vectors capture some accent and voice quality characteristics that get mixed with severity information, making perfect separation difficult. The system primarily models severity through timing changes and does not yet capture articulatory imprecision — the distorted sound formation that is a primary feature of glossectomy speech. Evaluating naturalness and severity in pathological speech is inherently difficult because listeners struggle to separate the two perceptually.

title: Statistical Text-to-Speech Synthesis Based on Segment-Wise Representation With a Norm Constraint
summary: This paper addresses a well-known weakness in statistical text-to-speech systems: the synthesized speech sounds muffled or flat because the speech features are over-smoothed. In real speech, the acoustic features change dynamically from frame to frame, but statistical models tend to produce overly averaged-out feature sequences. The authors propose two connected improvements — a new way of representing the speech model that gives more flexibility in feature generation, and a mathematical constraint that forces the generated speech features to have a larger magnitude, closer to natural speech. Together these make the synthesized speech sound more natural.
methodology: Traditional HMM-based text-to-speech systems generate speech by finding the most likely sequence of acoustic features given a trained model. Each spoken sound state is replicated across every frame it occupies, which limits how much variation is possible. The proposed segment-wise representation changes this: instead of making every frame match the model exactly, it only requires the average of all frames within a sound segment to match the model mean. This frees up variation within the segment. Additionally, the authors observed that statistically generated features have a systematically smaller mathematical size (norm) compared to natural speech features. They add a penalty term to the cost function that gradually pushes the feature vector's norm up to match natural speech, using an iterative gradient descent algorithm that decreases this push over iterations as the model fit becomes the main priority.
findings: Listening tests showed a clear improvement. In a mean opinion score test with 20 listeners rating 9 sentences, the proposed method improved naturalness by more than one full point on a 5-point scale compared to the conventional statistical system. In a direct comparison test with 11 sentences and 20 listeners, the proposed method was preferred in 91.6% of cases, while the conventional approach was preferred in only 1% of cases. The proposed method achieved similar quality to the global variance approach — another technique for reducing over-smoothing — but with lower memory usage and lower computational cost.
conclusion: The combination of segment-wise model representation and norm constraint effectively reduces the over-smoothing problem in statistical TTS without requiring major changes to the underlying HMM model. The method gives the feature generation algorithm more room to produce natural variation while still staying statistically consistent with the trained model.
