Because it is more memory-efficient and computationally lighter than competing approaches, it is practical for deployment in real systems. The authors are also exploring combining this with concatenative TTS for further quality gains.
limitations: The method uses an empirically determined enhancement factor that must be estimated from a set of generated utterances per voice, requiring some tuning for each new voice. The iterative gradient descent process adds computational overhead during synthesis, though it is lighter than competing methods. The approach focuses on improving spectral dynamics but does not directly address prosody modeling. Performance may vary across different languages or voice types since the norm enhancement factor is voice-specific.

title: Agentic AI Arrives: How Gen Z Adopts Autonomous AI Agents
summary: As artificial intelligence evolves from reactive tools to autonomous systems capable of independently pursuing complex goals, understanding user adoption becomes critical. This study investigates how Generation Z in Germany perceives and adopts these new "Agentic AI" systems. Despite being digital natives, only about half of the surveyed participants had used AI agents, though many expressed interest. The research sought to uncover what drives their willingness to use such systems, focusing on factors like perceived usefulness, perceived humanness, risk, and trust. By analyzing survey data from 243 participants, the study provides valuable insights into the expectations and hesitations of a generation that will significantly influence the future of human-AI interaction, emphasizing that practical value often outweighs privacy concerns for these early adopters.
methodology: The researchers conducted an online survey in Germany, gathering 243 valid responses from Gen Z adults. They utilized Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) to test the cause-and-effect relationships between different factors, such as trust, perceived risk, and usefulness. Additionally, they applied Necessary Condition Analysis (NCA) to identify which factors are absolutely essential for adoption to occur. Participants evaluated concepts like "perceived humanness" and "intention to use" using a standard rating scale after reading a clear explanation and infographic detailing what Agentic AI is.
findings: The study found that "perceived usefulness" is the absolute strongest driver of a person's intention to use AI agents, meaning Gen Z prioritizes clear efficiency gains. Interestingly, while "perceived risk" negatively impacted trust and directly reduced the intention to use, a lack of trust itself did not significantly stop people from wanting to use the AI. Furthermore, the NCA results demonstrated that both perceived usefulness and a sense of humanness are necessary baseline conditions; without these two elements, user adoption simply will not happen.
conclusion: The research concludes that Gen Z is highly pragmatic when it comes to Agentic AI. They are willing to adopt autonomous agents if the technology offers clear, tangible benefits that improve their productivity. While they hold legitimate concerns about data privacy and system reliability, these worries are overshadowed by the functional value the AI provides. To encourage widespread adoption, developers should prioritize making AI agents genuinely useful and subtly human-like, while policymakers must ensure transparent data practices to protect young users from potential misuse.
limitations: The study relies entirely on self-reported survey data, which reflects what people say they will do rather than their actual real-world behavior. The voluntary nature of the survey might introduce non-response bias, as those more interested in AI were likelier to participate. Future research should gather actual usage data, explore different types of AI application domains, and expand the sample beyond Germany to test global trends.

title: Agentic AI: Autonomous Intelligence for Complex Goals-A Comprehensive Survey
summary: This comprehensive survey explores "Agentic AI," a new paradigm in artificial intelligence where systems autonomously pursue complex, multi-step goals with minimal human intervention. Unlike traditional AI, which excels at specific, rule-based tasks, or generative AI, which merely creates content, Agentic AI can dynamically plan, adapt, and make decisions in unpredictable environments. The paper highlights the core architectures, learning paradigms, and recent advancements like memory and tool use that make these systems possible. Furthermore, it outlines the transformative potential of Agentic AI across various industries, such as healthcare, finance, and manufacturing. Crucially, the survey stresses the urgent need for robust ethical frameworks to manage the unique risks associated with highly autonomous machines operating in society.
methodology: The authors systematically review the foundational concepts, technical architectures, and evaluation methods of Agentic AI. They break down development methodologies into specific architectural approaches, such as multi-agent systems and goal-oriented modules, alongside learning paradigms like reinforcement learning. The study analyzes recent advancements, including Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), episodic memory mechanisms, and tool integration strategies. Furthermore, the authors critically evaluate real-world case studies and standard benchmark datasets used to test these systems, creating a structured taxonomy to compare Agentic AI directly against classical AI agents.
findings: The review finds that Agentic AI significantly outperforms traditional models in adaptability and long-term goal achievement by effectively leveraging real-time data, memory, and external tools. Successful deployments in healthcare monitoring, adaptive algorithmic trading, and software engineering demonstrate its immense practical value. However, the study also reveals that these systems currently face massive operational challenges regarding scalability, computational resource consumption, and reliability when dealing with out-of-distribution events or complex environments where goals might suddenly become misaligned with human intentions.
conclusion: Agentic AI represents a massive leap forward, moving machines from being passive assistants to active, goal-driven collaborators. Its ability to handle dynamic, unstructured problems promises to revolutionize productivity and problem-solving across society. However, realizing this immense potential requires overcoming significant technical and ethical hurdles. The survey emphasizes that future progress relies not just on building better algorithms, but on developing universal ethical standards, transparent governance, and reliable alignment techniques to ensure these autonomous systems remain highly beneficial and structurally safe.
limitations: The survey identifies several limitations in the current state of Agentic AI, including the extreme difficulty of aligning AI goals with human values. High computational resource demands and vulnerability to dynamic environmental shifts remain problematic. Evaluating these systems is also incredibly challenging due to the lack of standardized benchmarks for open-ended tasks. Future research must address decentralized coordination and create real-time regulatory frameworks.

title: Agentic AI Security: Threats, Defenses, Evaluation, and Open Challenges
summary: As Agentic AI systems—powered by Large Language Models equipped with memory, planning, and tool use—become more autonomous, they introduce severe and unprecedented security risks. This survey provides a comprehensive analysis of the unique vulnerabilities facing Agentic AI, differentiating them from traditional software and standard AI safety issues. Because these agents can independently interact with the web, execute code, and communicate with other agents, they are susceptible to advanced attacks like indirect prompt injection, autonomous cyber-exploitation, and protocol-level threats. The paper organizes these threats into a detailed taxonomy, reviews current defense strategies, and outlines the urgent need for better benchmarking and secure-by-design principles to protect society from autonomous AI misuse.
methodology: The authors construct a structured taxonomy classifying security threats into distinct categories: Prompt Injection and Jailbreaks, Autonomous Cyber-Exploitation, Multi-Agent and Protocol-Level Threats, and Interface Risks. They systematically analyze state-of-the-art defense mechanisms, categorizing them into agent-focused interventions, user-focused human verification, and system-focused sandboxing strategies. Finally, they review existing security benchmarks to evaluate how well current systems withstand sophisticated attacks. The methodology involves synthesizing literature across these domains to highlight gaps where current defenses fail against adaptive, multi-step exploits, framing the security challenge from a threat actor's perspective.
findings: The research highlights that traditional security measures are entirely insufficient for Agentic AI. Attackers can easily hijack agents using indirect prompt injections hidden in websites, forcing the AI to steal data or execute malware. Multi-agent systems amplify these risks, as compromised agents can infect others or manipulate shared protocols without detection. While defenses like sandboxing, prompt augmentation, and alignment training offer some protection, they often reduce the agent's usefulness or fail against adaptive, multi-step attacks. Furthermore, agents show extreme fragility when operating in realistic, dynamic web environments.
conclusion: Securing Agentic AI is an incredibly complex and unsolved problem. Because these systems act autonomously over long periods and connect to external tools, their attack surface is massive. The paper concludes that no single defense is adequate; securing these agents requires a multi-layered approach combining robust training, strict execution sandboxes, and continuous real-time monitoring. Ultimately, the deployment of agentic systems in high-stakes areas must be paired with stringent governance and entirely new evaluation frameworks that strictly test long-term, multi-step security resilience.
limitations: Current defense mechanisms often degrade the general capabilities of the AI and struggle to scale efficiently in multi-agent environments. Existing security benchmarks mostly test short-term tasks, failing to capture the risks that emerge over long, complex agent workflows. Future research must focus on developing formal safety guarantees, improving temporal robustness across multiple steps, and creating decentralized security protocols for multi-agent ecosystems to prevent cascading failures.

title: Vector Field Decomposition-Based Flow Matching for Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Text-to-Speech
summary: Zero-shot cross-lingual Text-to-Speech (TTS) models aim to generate speech in a target language using the voice of a speaker who was never seen during training, based only on a short audio sample. A major challenge is that the speech prompt contains not just the speaker's voice characteristics, but also the pronunciation rules of the language they were speaking. This linguistic interference confuses the model. To solve this, the authors propose a novel "DecoFlow matching" technique. This method cleverly separates the speaker's voice identity from the language information within the model's generation process, allowing for highly accurate, natural-sounding cross-lingual speech synthesis without the original language corrupting the output.
methodology: The core of the approach is "vector field decomposition" within the flow matching framework. The model splits the predictive vector field, which guides how noise transforms into speech, into two distinct components: a language component and a speaker component, using two separate U-Net neural networks. A specialized mathematical loss function forces these two components to be mathematically orthogonal and activates them in completely different frequency bands. Speaker information is heavily weighted in the lower frequencies, while language information dominates the higher frequencies, reflecting natural human speech patterns.
findings: The proposed Deco-TTS model successfully disentangled speaker and language information effectively. Visualizing the model's inner workings proved that the speaker component correctly activated heavily in the lower frequency ranges while being heavily suppressed in the higher ranges. In formal evaluations using English, French, and German, Deco-TTS consistently outperformed a strong conventional model in both subjective human evaluations for naturalness and objective metrics for pronunciation accuracy and speaker similarity, proving it generates much more accurate and realistic cross-lingual speech.
conclusion: By explicitly separating speaker and language attributes into different frequency bands during the flow matching process, the DecoFlow matching technique resolves the inherent conflict in cross-lingual TTS tasks. This decomposition allows the model to fully utilize the rich voice information present in a speech prompt without letting the prompt's original language corrupt the target language's pronunciation. This represents a highly significant advancement in creating flexible, realistic, and globally applicable voice cloning technologies that transcend basic language barriers.
limitations: The paper relies on specific frequency band separations, which might vary slightly across extremely diverse languages or unusual vocal characteristics. It also notes the difficulty of comparing against commercial models whose official code is unreleased, limiting comparisons to available demo samples. Future work might need to explore how this precise vector decomposition scales to highly tonal languages or noisy, low-quality speech prompts in real-world scenarios.

title: Exploring Natural Language Processing in Model-To-Model Transformations
summary: This paper investigates how natural language processing (NLP) can help automate and improve "model-to-model" transformations in software engineering. When developers create visual diagrams (like UML or process models), the text labels are often short and tricky to process. The researchers wanted to see if modern AI could automatically read these short labels to extract action-object pairs (like "send invoice"), understand complex sentences using "and" or "or", and correctly identify abbreviations. By solving this, computers could automatically convert one type of software diagram into another, saving developers a lot of manual work and reducing errors.
methodology: The authors tested several popular NLP tools (like Spacy, Stanza, and BERT) and built their own custom deep-learning models (BERT-BiLSTM-CRF). They used regular expression rules to pull out noun and verb phrases from the text. To handle complex sentences with words like "and" or "or," they used dependency parsing, which maps out how words in a sentence relate to each other. Finally, they trained a machine-learning classifier called CatBoost to look at the context of a word and predict whether it was an abbreviation or an acronym.
findings: The Stanza toolkit performed best overall for extracting phrases, but the authors' custom-trained model was slightly better at specifically finding verb phrases. The CatBoost classifier proved very accurate at spotting abbreviations based on surrounding words. However, breaking down complex sentences with "and" and "or" was extremely difficult; the tools struggled because changing just one word drastically changed how the computer parsed the entire sentence, leading to low accuracy in that specific task.
conclusion: Using advanced NLP tools is a highly effective way to extract useful information from the short text labels used in software diagrams. This automation can significantly speed up how software models are translated and built. While extracting basic phrases and spotting abbreviations worked well, the research shows that current AI still struggles with complex, poorly formatted grammar. The techniques developed here are not just for software models but could also help in other fields like process mining and building chatbots.
limitations: The tools struggled significantly when human modelers used bad naming practices, such as writing full sentences instead of short labels or using inconsistent capitalization. Furthermore, the datasets currently available for training AI are built for long documents, not the short, ambiguous phrases found in diagrams. Finally, while the system can detect abbreviations, automatically expanding them into their full words remains an unsolved challenge.

title: Exploring New Frontiers in Agricultural NLP: Investigating the Potential of Large Language Models for Food Applications
summary: This study explores how advanced artificial intelligence can be tailored for the agricultural and food sectors. A major challenge in food policy is linking the short, messy product descriptions from grocery store receipts to detailed, scientific national nutrition databases. General AI models struggle with this specific vocabulary. To fix this, the researchers built "AgriBERT," a custom language model trained exclusively on agricultural research. They compared this specialized model against massive, general-purpose models like GPT-4 and Claude 3 to see which approach best matched store items to their correct nutritional profiles and predicted recipe cuisines.
methodology: The researchers collected over 46,000 agricultural research papers to teach their custom model, AgriBERT, the specific language of food science from scratch. To make the model even smarter, they linked the food descriptions to a giant, structured food dictionary called the "FoodOn" knowledge graph. They also used GPT-4 to generate alternative, rephrased versions of the descriptions to give the model more varied examples to learn from. The task was set up like a multiple-choice test, where the AI had to pick the correct nutritional description for a given grocery item.
findings: When trained purely on agricultural data, AgriBERT significantly outperformed general-purpose models in correctly matching grocery items to nutritional data. Feeding the model extra clues from the FoodOn dictionary and GPT-4 further boosted its accuracy, helping it pick the right answer as its top choice much more often. In a separate test where the AI had to predict the cultural cuisine of a recipe based solely on its ingredients, AgriBERT again beat out traditional algorithms, proving its strong understanding of food contexts.
conclusion: Creating a specialized AI model like AgriBERT is highly effective for solving specific problems in agriculture and food science, outperforming general models that lack domain expertise. Furthermore, combining structured databases (like food knowledge graphs) with the reasoning abilities of large language models is a powerful strategy. This technology can successfully bridge the gap between everyday grocery shopping data and scientific nutritional research, ultimately helping policymakers track public health and dietary habits more accurately.
limitations: While giant models like GPT-4 perform well, they require massive amounts of computing power, making them less practical for everyday use compared to smaller, focused models like AgriBERT. Additionally, the system sometimes got confused when linking data; for example, if an external database connected a food description to a brand name rather than the actual food type, it lowered the model's overall matching accuracy.

title: Leverage NLP Models Against Other NLP Models: Two Invisible Feature Space Backdoor Attacks
summary: This paper reveals a major security flaw in artificial intelligence systems that process language. Hackers can perform "backdoor attacks" by sneaking poisoned data into a model's training set. The model behaves perfectly normally most of the time, but if it sees a secret "trigger," it intentionally makes a mistake. Previously, these triggers were obvious things like a weirdly placed rare word. To make the attacks invisible to security scanners, the authors created two new methods that use the invisible "fingerprints" left behind by AI text-generation tools (like specific writing styles or paraphrasing patterns) as the hidden triggers.
methodology: The researchers developed two attacks: "Multi-StyleBkd" and "Paraphrase." For the style attack, they used an AI tool to rewrite normal training sentences into five distinct styles (like Shakespearean or poetic) and assigned them a malicious label. For the paraphrase attack, they simply used a GPT-2 model to rewrite sentences in a natural way. They then trained victim AI models on this poisoned data. Finally, they tested if they could trick the models into outputting the wrong answer simply by feeding them new sentences that had been restyled or paraphrased by the same tools.
findings: Both attacks successfully tricked the victim AI models. The multi-style attack achieved over an 84% success rate, and the paraphrase attack succeeded over 90% of the time. Crucially, when the researchers ran standard security defenses designed to catch awkward grammar or suspicious words, the defenses failed completely. Because the triggers were just natural-sounding AI-generated phrasing, the poisoned sentences looked perfectly normal and kept their original meaning, allowing the attack to remain completely undetected.
conclusion: AI-generated text can be easily weaponized to create highly stealthy backdoor triggers. Because modern language models naturally leave behind subtle, invisible patterns when they rewrite text, hackers can teach victim models to react to these patterns. This exposes a severe security vulnerability for anyone downloading and using AI models or training data from untrusted sources on the internet, showing that current defense mechanisms are entirely unequipped to stop sophisticated, feature-based attacks.
limitations: For the attacks to be highly reliable, the hacker needs to poison up to 20% of the training data, which might be noticed by an observant developer. Furthermore, if a normal user accidentally types a sentence that naturally resembles the trigger style or phrasing, they might accidentally activate the backdoor and get a wrong answer. Finally, rewriting text into multiple styles is complex and occasionally introduces slight grammatical errors.

title: On the Evaluation of NLP-based Models for Software Engineering
summary: Artificial intelligence that understands natural language is increasingly being used to solve software engineering problems, such as automatically summarizing code, finding bugs, or generating tests. However, evaluating whether these AI models actually do a good job has become a messy problem. This review paper examines recent research to see exactly how scientists are testing their AI models in software engineering. The authors set out to determine if the current testing methods are fair, consistent, and truly measuring the model's helpfulness to real programmers.
methodology: The researchers conducted a systematic literature review, searching through top-tier software engineering conferences and journals published over the last five years. They narrowed their search down to 104 relevant papers that specifically applied natural language AI to software tasks. They then read through these papers to categorize and analyze the evaluation metrics being used, dividing them into two main types: automatic mathematical scoring systems (like accuracy or text-matching scores) and manual evaluations (where human experts grade the AI's output).
findings: The review revealed a chaotic evaluation landscape with no standard rules. While automatic metrics are heavily used because they are fast and easy, they often fail to capture the true quality or usefulness of the AI's output. To fix this, researchers rely on human experts, but they use wildly inconsistent terminology. Different papers use words like "usefulness," "naturalness," "readability," and "informativeness" to mean entirely different things, and they grade them on completely different scales, making it impossible to compare one paper's results to another.
conclusion: The software engineering community urgently needs a standardized, methodical framework for testing AI models. Because researchers are currently inventing their own testing rules and definitions on the fly, it is incredibly difficult to know if a new AI model is actually better than an old one. Establishing clear, consistent metrics—especially for human evaluations—is essential to ensure that future advancements in the field are genuinely measured, validated, and practically useful for developers.
limitations: As a short workshop review, this paper successfully highlights the severe inconsistencies in how AI models are tested, but it does not provide a finalized, ready-to-use framework to fix the problem. The authors note that standardizing human evaluation is inherently difficult because it relies on subjective opinions, and they leave the development of a strict new evaluation protocol for future research.

title: Agentic AI Service Architecture Based on SOA
summary: This paper explains how to build intelligent AI systems using Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA). Instead of creating one large system, it breaks tasks into smaller services that can work together. An AI agent acts like a manager that decides which services to use and in what order. The system also uses digital identity (DID) and verifiable credentials to ensure secure and trustworthy interactions between agents. A shared workspace called a blackboard allows multiple agents to collaborate and solve problems together. The approach improves flexibility, scalability, and security. The paper demonstrates this idea using a prototype that shows how multiple agents can securely cooperate to solve complex tasks.
methodology: The system treats each AI agent as a reusable service with a defined interface. When a task arrives, the agent divides it into smaller subtasks and assigns them to different services or agents. It follows a two-phase process: first, it verifies agents using digital identity and grants limited permissions; second, agents collaborate on a shared blackboard by posting ideas and refining solutions. Communication is secured using DID-based credentials. The architecture also tracks inputs and outputs to monitor performance and decisions. This modular design allows agents to dynamically choose tools and coordinate actions instead of following fixed instructions.
findings: The results show that combining SOA with agent-based systems improves scalability, flexibility, and collaboration. The proof-of-concept demonstrates that agents can securely interact using decentralized identity while maintaining controlled access. The blackboard approach allows multiple agents to contribute partial solutions, leading to better problem-solving. The system also enables tracking of agent behavior, making it easier to understand decisions and performance. Overall, the architecture successfully supports multi-agent cooperation while maintaining security and transparency.
conclusion: The paper concludes that integrating SOA with agentic AI is an effective way to build scalable and secure AI systems. By treating agents as independent services and using decentralized identity, the system ensures flexibility and trust. The blackboard model enhances collaboration between agents, enabling them to solve complex tasks efficiently. This approach supports modular design, easier maintenance, and better interoperability across systems. It provides a strong foundation for future AI systems that require coordination between multiple agents.
limitations: The work mainly demonstrates a prototype and does not fully evaluate large-scale real-world deployment. Performance under heavy workloads or highly dynamic environments is not deeply analyzed. The reliance on DID and credential systems may introduce complexity in implementation. Additionally, coordination strategies between agents could be further optimized, and more comparisons with other architectures are needed.

title: Agentic AI With Orchestrator-Agent Trust: A Modular Visual Classification Framework With Trust-Aware Orchestration and RAG-Based Reasoning
summary: This paper addresses a key problem in AI: how to trust decisions made by multiple AI agents, especially when they are not trained for a specific task. The authors propose a system where different AI agents analyze images and an orchestrator evaluates their answers. The system also uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to improve decisions by comparing with similar past examples. It is tested on apple leaf disease classification. The framework separates visual understanding from decision-making, making the system more interpretable. The results show significant improvement in accuracy and reliability, proving that combining trust evaluation with retrieval-based reasoning leads to better AI performance.
methodology: The system uses multiple visual AI agents to classify images and provide confidence scores and explanations. A central orchestrator analyzes these outputs and decides the final result. It uses trust metrics like calibration error and overconfidence to judge reliability. If confidence is low or inconsistent, the system retrieves similar images using CLIP and re-evaluates the decision (RAG loop). The framework is tested in three stages: zero-shot (no training), fine-tuned models, and trust-aware orchestration with RAG. This layered approach improves decision quality step by step.
findings: The system achieved major improvements in accuracy, especially in zero-shot conditions. Accuracy increased from about 48% to over 85% using trust-aware orchestration and RAG. The study also found that some models are overconfident, which can lead to errors. The orchestrator successfully corrected such issues by re-evaluating predictions. The use of retrieval-based reasoning helped ground decisions in real examples, improving both accuracy and interpretability. Overall, separating reasoning and perception improved system reliability.
conclusion: The paper shows that trust-aware orchestration is critical for reliable multi-agent AI systems. By combining multiple agents, confidence evaluation, and retrieval-based reasoning, the system becomes more accurate and explainable. The modular design allows scalability and adaptability across domains. This approach represents a shift from single-model AI to collaborative agent-based systems, especially for high-risk applications like diagnostics.
limitations: The system is tested on a specific dataset (apple leaf diseases), so generalization to other domains needs further validation. The approach increases computational complexity due to multiple agents and re-evaluation loops. Dependence on external retrieval systems may introduce latency. Also, trust metrics may not fully capture all types of model errors.

title: Expert Agents for Social and Emotional Learning: An Agentic Graph Retrieval Augmented Generation Approach
summary: This paper presents an AI system called MySELAgent that helps teachers apply Social and Emotional Learning (SEL) in classrooms. It uses agent-based AI, knowledge graphs, and retrieval techniques to provide personalized recommendations for students. The system can assess student emotional needs, suggest suitable activities, and create learning plans. It combines structured data (knowledge graphs) with language models to generate accurate and context-aware responses. The goal is to support teachers who lack training or time to implement SEL effectively. The system aims to improve education by making SEL easier to apply and more personalized.
methodology: The system uses multiple AI agents that interact with two knowledge graphs: one containing SEL theory and another containing student emotional data. When a teacher provides input, the system retrieves relevant information from these graphs and uses language models to generate responses (Graph RAG). Agents collaborate to analyze needs, recommend activities, and create learning pathways. The system also connects with external tools for assessment. This multi-agent setup allows dynamic decision-making and reduces errors compared to static AI systems.
findings: The results show that combining knowledge graphs with RAG improves the accuracy and relevance of AI responses. The system provides personalized recommendations and supports multi-step reasoning. It also reduces hallucinations compared to standard language models. Evaluations using different models show consistent and reliable performance. The approach demonstrates that agent-based systems can effectively support complex educational tasks.
conclusion: The paper concludes that Agentic Graph RAG is a powerful approach for educational AI systems. By combining structured knowledge, retrieval, and multi-agent reasoning, the system delivers accurate and personalized support. It helps teachers implement SEL more effectively and consistently. The modular design allows integration with other tools and future expansion.
limitations: The system relies on high-quality data in knowledge graphs, which may not always be available. Privacy concerns arise due to handling sensitive student emotional data. Real-world deployment in diverse classrooms is not fully tested. The evaluation uses partly synthetic data, which may not reflect real scenarios completely.

title: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications
summary: This paper explains how AI is evolving from large models (like language models) to agent-based systems, especially for future 6G communication networks. Traditional AI models are powerful but passive, meaning they only respond to inputs. Agentic AI, on the other hand, can actively make decisions, plan tasks, and adapt to changing environments. The paper describes how these systems can improve communication networks by managing resources, optimizing performance, and handling complex tasks automatically. It also reviews the development of AI models and explains how combining them with agent-based design leads to smarter and more flexible systems.
methodology: The paper presents a conceptual framework where large AI models are combined with agent components like planners, memory, tools, and knowledge bases. These components work together in a loop: perceive the environment, reason about it, plan actions, and execute them. It also explains different learning methods such as pre-training, fine-tuning, and retrieval-based learning. The system can manage communication tasks like scheduling and resource allocation by continuously learning and adapting.
findings: The study finds that agent-based AI significantly improves adaptability and decision-making compared to traditional models. It can handle dynamic environments like 6G networks more effectively. Agentic AI enables real-time optimization, better resource management, and improved system efficiency. It also supports complex multi-agent collaboration and task execution, which are difficult for static models.
conclusion: The paper concludes that Agentic AI is the next step in AI evolution, especially for intelligent communication systems. By combining reasoning, planning, and learning, these systems can operate autonomously in complex environments. This shift is essential for future technologies like 6G, where systems must adapt quickly and manage resources efficiently.
limitations: The paper is mainly a tutorial and does not provide detailed experimental validation. Practical implementation challenges such as computational cost and system complexity are not deeply explored. Integration with real-world communication infrastructure may require further research and testing.

title: A Study on the Translation of Spoken English from Speech to Text
summary: This paper focuses on improving spoken English translation by breaking the problem into two steps: converting speech into text and then translating the text. Instead of directly translating audio, which is difficult, the authors use machine learning models to simplify the process. A convolutional neural network (CNN) is used for speech recognition, while a long short-term memory (LSTM) model is used for translation. The goal is to improve accuracy and efficiency in real-time communication. Experiments show that this combined approach performs better than traditional methods, especially in handling noise and improving translation quality.
methodology: The system works in two stages. First, speech is converted into text using a CNN. Audio is divided into small frames, features like MFCC are extracted, and the CNN predicts characters. Then, the text is translated using an encoder-decoder model based on LSTM. The encoder converts text into a vector (meaning representation), and the decoder generates the translated sentence. A bidirectional LSTM is used to better understand context. This two-step approach simplifies the complex task of direct speech translation.
findings: The results show that CNN performs better than traditional models like HMM and BP neural networks for speech recognition. Similarly, LSTM performs better than standard RNNs for translation tasks. When combined, the system achieves higher accuracy and better noise resistance. The approach also improves real-time translation efficiency. Overall, dividing the task into two stages leads to better performance than end-to-end speech translation.
conclusion: The paper concludes that separating speech recognition and translation is more effective than handling both together. Using CNN for speech-to-text and LSTM for translation improves accuracy, robustness, and efficiency. This method is practical for real-world communication systems where speed and clarity are important.
limitations: The study relies on simulation experiments rather than large-scale real-world deployment. It does not explore advanced end-to-end models, which may outperform the two-step approach in some cases. The system may still struggle with highly complex or noisy speech in real environments.

title: Emotional Text-To-Speech in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset
summary: This paper studies how to generate emotional speech using artificial datasets instead of human recordings. The focus is on Japanese, where emotional speech data is limited. The authors use synthetic data generated by voice software to train a speech synthesis model. The goal is to see if artificial data can replace real emotional speech data. Results show that artificial datasets can produce reasonable emotional speech but struggle with subtle or complex emotions. The work highlights both the potential and limitations of using synthetic data in emotional speech systems.
methodology: The system uses a text-to-speech model (Tacotron 2) trained on artificially generated emotional speech data from software like Voiceroid. The model converts text into mel-spectrograms, which are then turned into audio using a vocoder (WaveGlow). The output is evaluated using objective metrics like MCD, SDR, and F0 error, and subjective human ratings (MOS). The study compares synthetic speech with original outputs to measure quality, naturalness, and emotional expression.
findings: The results show that artificial datasets can generate speech with basic emotional expressions, but they are less natural than real human speech. Objective metrics indicate moderate distortion and errors in pitch and sound quality. Human evaluations show that emotionally aligned sentences (matching text and tone) perform better. However, subtle emotions and mixed feelings are not well captured. Overall, the system works but still has noticeable quality gaps.
conclusion: The paper concludes that artificial datasets are a useful alternative when real emotional data is scarce. They can support the development of emotional speech systems, especially for low-resource languages. However, improvements are needed to match human-level expressiveness and naturalness.
limitations: The biggest limitation is the inability of synthetic data to capture complex and subtle emotions. The model also shows inconsistency in pitch and speech quality. Results depend heavily on dataset quality, and real-world performance may vary. More advanced models and better datasets are needed.

title: Joint Speech-Text Embeddings for Multitask Speech Processing
summary: This paper proposes a system that combines speech and text processing into a single model. Instead of building separate systems for speech recognition, text-to-speech, and speaker recognition, the model learns all tasks together. It uses a shared representation (embedding) for both speech and text, allowing better learning and reduced memory usage. The goal is to make speech systems more efficient and suitable for devices with limited resources. The results show that the model performs well across multiple tasks while using less memory.
methodology: The system uses a shared encoder to process both speech and text inputs into a common representation. A speech encoder extracts features from audio, and a text encoder processes text. These are aligned and passed through a shared encoder. Then, different decoders handle different tasks: ASR (speech-to-text), TTS (text-to-speech), and speaker recognition. The model is trained on all tasks simultaneously, allowing it to learn shared features efficiently.
findings: The model achieves strong performance across tasks: high accuracy in speaker recognition (~97%), reasonable error rates in speech recognition, and acceptable speech synthesis quality. It also reduces memory usage compared to separate models. Additionally, the system can perform voice conversion even without explicit training for it, showing flexibility.
conclusion: The paper concludes that combining speech and text tasks into a single model is efficient and effective. Shared learning improves performance while reducing resource usage. This approach is especially useful for real-world applications like smart devices where memory and computation are limited.
limitations: The model’s performance is slightly lower than specialized single-task models in some cases. Training multiple tasks together can introduce complexity and interference. The system may also require careful tuning to balance different tasks effectively.

title: Towards Energy-Preserving Natural Language Understanding With Spiking Neural Networks
summary: This paper addresses the problem of high energy consumption in modern AI models used for language understanding. Traditional neural networks require many complex calculations, making them inefficient for devices like smartphones. The authors propose using spiking neural networks (SNNs), which mimic the human brain and use simpler operations. These networks process information as spikes instead of continuous values, reducing energy usage. The goal is to maintain performance while drastically lowering power consumption.
methodology: The model replaces traditional neural network layers with spiking neural network layers. Instead of using multiplication-heavy operations, it uses simpler addition-based computations. The system converts inputs into spike signals and processes them through multiple layers. To make training possible, approximation techniques are used for backpropagation. The model is tested on tasks like sentiment analysis and machine translation.
findings: The results show that the proposed model achieves performance similar to standard Transformer models while using only about 0.82% of the energy. This is a significant reduction in computational cost. The model also outperforms other energy-saving methods like knowledge distillation in terms of efficiency.
conclusion: The paper concludes that spiking neural networks are a promising solution for energy-efficient AI. They can maintain competitive performance while drastically reducing power consumption. This makes them suitable for deployment in low-power devices and real-world applications.
limitations: The approach is still experimental and not widely adopted in real systems. Training SNNs is more complex due to their discrete nature. The model may also struggle with scalability and integration into existing architectures.

title: A Unified Understanding of Deep NLP Models for Text Classification
summary: This paper focuses on understanding how different deep learning models (like CNN, LSTM, and Transformers) work for text classification. These models are powerful but complex, making them hard to interpret and debug. The authors propose a unified way to analyze them using a tool called DeepNLPVis. Instead of looking at each model separately, the tool explains how words and phrases contribute to predictions across different layers. It helps developers identify errors, understand model behavior, and improve performance. The key idea is to track how information flows through the model and how each word affects the final decision.
methodology: The approach uses a measure based on mutual information to track how much each word contributes to the final prediction. It analyzes both individual words (intra-word) and relationships between words (inter-word, like phrases). The system provides three levels of analysis: dataset level (overall performance), sample level (specific sentences), and word level (individual word importance). By adding noise and observing changes in predictions, the model estimates how important each word is. This unified framework works across different NLP architectures.
findings: The study shows that the tool can effectively reveal why models make correct or incorrect predictions. It helps identify issues like misinterpreted words, confusing phrases, and data problems. It also allows comparison between models, showing that simpler models can sometimes perform competitively with complex ones. The visualization makes it easier to debug and improve models.
conclusion: The paper concludes that a unified interpretation framework is essential for understanding and improving deep NLP models. By analyzing word contributions and relationships across layers, developers can better diagnose problems and optimize models. This approach improves transparency and supports better decision-making in model design.
limitations: The approach relies on visualization, which may not scale well for very large datasets. It also requires additional computation to estimate word importance. The method focuses mainly on text classification and may need adaptation for other NLP tasks.

title: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts: A Comparative Study of GPT With Human Experts and Machine Learning
summary: This paper studies how well AI (GPT-4) can generate meaningful messages about life events like birth, death, hiring, and firing. The main question is whether AI-generated messages match the intent of the prompt. The researchers use structured prompts to guide the AI and then evaluate the results using human experts and machine learning models. They generate 24,000 messages and analyze their quality. The study finds that most AI-generated messages are valid and match the intended meaning, showing that prompt design plays a key role in improving AI-generated text.
methodology: The researchers use structured narrative prompts (SNPs) to generate messages with GPT-4. A sample of messages is manually labeled by human reviewers as valid or invalid. Then, multiple machine learning models (like BERT, SVM, and Random Forest) are trained on this labeled data. These models classify the remaining messages, and an ensemble approach combines their predictions. The system compares AI outputs with human judgments to measure accuracy and alignment.
findings: The results show that about 87% of AI-generated messages match the intended prompt when evaluated by humans. Machine learning models achieve around 90% agreement with human evaluations. The study also finds that some types of events (like hiring) are easier for AI to generate correctly than others (like birth). Ensemble models improve classification reliability, and structured prompts significantly enhance output quality.
conclusion: The paper concludes that AI can generate high-quality, contextually accurate messages when guided by well-designed prompts. Structured prompts improve consistency, clarity, and alignment with user intent. Combining human evaluation with machine learning provides a scalable way to validate AI outputs.
limitations: The study focuses only on a limited set of life events and English text, which limits generalization. It also depends heavily on structured prompts, so results may differ with open-ended inputs. Some incorrect messages are still difficult to detect automatically.

title: Text-to-Speech With Lip Synchronization Based on Speech-Assisted Text-to-Video Alignment and Masked Unit Prediction
summary: This paper introduces STAMP, a smarter way to generate speech that perfectly matches the lip movements in a silent video, given only the text script. Older systems assumed every sound takes roughly the same time and struggled when lips were hidden or turned away. STAMP fixes this by using the real speech (available only during training) to guide better alignment between text and video frames, plus clever tricks to guess missing lip information from context. This leads to more natural, synchronized talking videos. It is very useful for dubbing foreign films, fixing bad audio in lectures, or animating characters.
methodology: The system encodes lip images and text, then aligns them using a new 'speech-assisted' loss (STA) that copies the exact timing from real speech via a pre-trained TTS model instead of forcing even spacing. For hidden lips, it randomly masks audio units during training so the model learns to predict them from surrounding frames (masked unit prediction). It also adds a 'null embedding' trick (like classifier-free guidance) so the model works even without clear lip images at test time. The final speech comes from discrete audio units fed to a vocoder.
findings: On chemistry lecture videos, STAMP beat previous systems (Neural Dubber and DSU-AVO) in lip-sync error measures and word error rate for speech clarity. Human listeners preferred STAMP's speech quality (+0.25 CMOS) and sync. It worked better even when lips were partly missing, proving the new losses really help.
conclusion: STAMP solves two big headaches in lip-synced speech synthesis—wrong timing assumptions and missing lip views—making video dubbing and audio repair much more reliable. The ideas can be added to other speech systems and open the door for better automatic voice-overs and animation.
limitations: Tested mainly on one male speaker's lecture videos, so more diverse speakers and scenes are needed. It still needs a pre-trained TTS model for alignment during training and may require extra data for completely new speaking styles.

title: Vec-Tok Speech: Speech Vectorization and Tokenization for Neural Speech Generation
summary: Vec-Tok is a flexible new framework for high-quality, expressive speech generation across many tasks like voice changing, text-to-speech, speech translation, and even cleaning noisy audio. It splits speech into two parts: continuous 'vectors' that keep every tiny sound detail for super clear output, and discrete 'tokens' that capture only the words and style for easy language-model processing. A clever compression step (BPE) makes training faster and more stable. Trained on 50,000 hours of data, it shines in zero-shot scenarios where you give just a few seconds of example speech.
methodology: The codec first uses WavLM to pull rich continuous vectors from audio for perfect reconstruction. It then normalizes them to remove speaker info and clusters them into semantic tokens using K-means. A small inverse K-means network plus a prompt vector turns tokens back into full vectors. A LLaMA-style language model predicts the BPE-compressed tokens for tasks like TTS (text + style prompt) or translation (source tokens). The decoder finally combines vectors and tokens through a HiFi-GAN vocoder.
findings: Vec-Tok outperformed state-of-the-art models in zero-shot voice conversion, style-transfer TTS, speech-to-speech translation, denoising, and speaker anonymization. Human ratings showed higher naturalness, style similarity, and speaker similarity. It handled cross-lingual tasks and noisy inputs well with only short prompts.
conclusion: By cleverly combining detailed vectors and compact tokens, Vec-Tok creates one powerful system for almost any speech task without retraining everything. It makes expressive, high-fidelity speech generation much more practical and flexible for real-world apps like dubbing or virtual assistants.
limitations: Requires a huge 50,000-hour dataset for best results, which may not be available for every language. Mostly tested on English and Chinese; performance on very low-resource languages is not fully explored. The language model still needs careful prompt design for best style control.

title: Textless Unit-to-Unit Training for Many-to-Many Multilingual Speech-to-Speech Translation
summary: This paper presents UTUT, a completely text-free way to train speech-to-speech translation that works across many languages at once. Instead of text, it uses simple 'speech units' (phoneme-like codes from a self-supervised model) as both input and output. By training one model to translate units from any source language to any target language (using language tags), it learns efficiently with far less data and can even handle brand-new language pairs never seen together before. The same model easily switches to text-to-speech or text-to-speech translation with tiny tweaks.
methodology: Speech is turned into discrete units using multilingual HuBERT + clustering (1,000 vocabulary). A transformer encoder-decoder is trained: the encoder sees source units plus a source-language tag, and the decoder predicts target units using a target-language tag. Training uses many-to-many parallel speech pairs from 19 languages. For text tasks, phonemes replace units as input. A unit-based vocoder turns output units back into audio.
findings: On Europarl-ST and CVSS benchmarks, UTUT matched or beat specialized single-direction models while using much less training data per language pair. It successfully translated unseen pairs and transferred smoothly to multilingual text-to-speech and text-to-speech translation. BLEU, COMET, and human MOS scores were strong even for low-resource directions.
conclusion: UTUT proves you can build powerful multilingual speech translation without any text at all, making it great for languages without writing systems. The many-to-many approach saves huge amounts of data and enables flexible zero-shot translation plus easy reuse for synthesis tasks.
limitations: Depends on good quality speech units from the pre-trained HuBERT model. Tested on European and some Asian languages; very distant or tonal languages may need extra tuning. Some performance drop occurs without extra fine-tuning when switching to new speech encoders.

title: The Impact of Knowledge Distillation on the Energy Consumption and Runtime Efficiency of NLP Models
summary: Big AI language models like BERT and GPT-2 are powerful but use lots of electricity and take time to run. This study checks if making smaller 'distilled' versions saves energy and runs faster while still working well. Researchers tested the original and distilled models on three common language tasks from the GLUE dataset. They measured real energy use, CPU and memory load, and how quickly each model answered questions on a normal server.
methodology: They compared four models: BERT, Distilled-BERT, GPT-2, and Distilled-GPT-2. Each ran the same three tasks (SST-2, STS-B, MNLI) ten times in a row on a Linux server with 32 GB RAM and an 8-core CPU. Energy was measured in joules using PowerJoular software, CPU and memory with Linux commands, and speed with Python timers. They used ANOVA and t-tests to check if differences were real, not just luck.
findings: Distilled models clearly used less energy: Distilled-BERT saved 44% and Distilled-GPT-2 saved 28% compared to the originals. They also answered faster and sometimes used less CPU. Memory savings were small for BERT but bigger for GPT-2. All differences were statistically significant, proving distillation really helps efficiency without big performance loss.
conclusion: Knowledge distillation makes NLP models greener and quicker, which is great for phones, laptops, or companies that care about electricity bills and the environment. Developers should pick distilled versions when speed and energy matter most.
limitations: Only tested two popular models and three tasks on one type of computer. Results might change with different hardware, more languages, or real-world apps. Future work should check long-term environmental impact and test many more distilled models.

title: Privacy-Preserving Deep Learning NLP Models for Cancer Registries
summary: Cancer registries collect private text reports but cannot share patient data because of privacy rules. This paper shows how to train one smart model to pull key cancer details (like tumor location or grade) from reports and safely share the model between registries without ever sending raw text or patient names. They use clever transfer-learning tricks plus a special public-only vocabulary so no private info leaks.
methodology: They built a multi-task CNN that reads full pathology reports and predicts six cancer features at once. Data from two real registries was split into four 'virtual' registries. They compared training alone, all data together, and three transfer-learning styles. For privacy, they created a vocabulary using only words from public medical texts (MIMIC-III and PubMed) so the model can be passed around safely.
findings: The privacy-safe transfer-learning models (especially the cyclic version) reached almost the same accuracy as training on all data together (micro-F1 0.823 vs 0.827). Both beat single-registry models by a lot. Dropping the embedding layer or using only public words kept privacy without hurting performance much.
conclusion: Registries can now team up to build better cancer-extraction AI without breaking privacy laws. This is the first time cyclic transfer learning was used on medical text. It opens the door for stronger models using data from many places while keeping patients safe.
limitations: Only two registries and one language were tested. The public vocabulary covered about 83% of words, so some rare terms are missing. Needs testing on more registries, different languages, and real hospital systems before full rollout.

title: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines for a Societal Well-Being Scoring and Monitoring System: Moroccan Use Case
summary: News headlines contain clues about how society feels, but Arabic news is rarely studied this way. This paper uses Moroccan headlines to spot main topics (politics, sports, education) and check if they feel positive or negative. They built a scoring system that turns daily news into a 'well-being score' to track quality of life over time. Deep learning with Arabic BERT embeddings gave the best results.
methodology: First, they used LDA topic modeling on 418,000 Moroccan headlines to group them into eight quality-of-life topics, then cleaned and balanced the data. For sentiment, they scraped Hibapress headlines plus likes/dislikes to label positive or negative. They compared classic machine-learning (SVM, Random Forest) with deep models (GRU, Bi-GRU) using TF-IDF or Arabic BERT/ASAFAYA word embeddings.
findings: The GRU model with ASAFAYA embeddings reached 94.74% accuracy on topic classification. For sentiment, Bi-GRU with ASAFAYA hit 90.23% accuracy. The new well-being scoring system successfully turns categorized headlines into daily, monthly, or quarterly scores showing public mood across life areas.
conclusion: Arabic news headlines are a rich, real-time source for understanding societal well-being in Morocco and other Arabic countries. Combining topic modeling with deep sentiment analysis creates a practical monitoring tool for governments and researchers to spot problems early.
limitations: Only Moroccan Modern Standard Arabic news was used; dialects and other Arab countries need testing. The scoring system is new and needs long-term validation. More diverse news sources and real-time updates would make it even stronger.
